11.3.29 Tests

Fully outfitted HEUs are to be subjected to the following tests in the presence of the Surveyor:

11.3.29(a)

Life support system tests as per the applicable requirements of Section 8.

11.3.29(b)

HEUs are to undergo functional tests. Satisfactory operation at the maximum allowable working pressure using the normal breathing gas is to be demonstrated for the life support systems, piping systems, electrical systems, communication systems, propulsion systems, etc.

11.3.29(c)

Launch test of the fully loaded HEUs using the dedicated launch systems that are installed on the dive support vessel/offshore facility.

11.3.29(d) (2020)

Additional Tests for HEUs designed for Surface/Underwater Operations:

i) Deadweight Survey and Lightship Measurement. The location, number and size of all items listed on the weight and balance spreadsheet are to be physically checked after construction and outfitting. Fully outfitted HEUs are to be weighed with a scale and the measured weights are to be compared to the total spreadsheet weights, compensating for any

fitting. Fully outfitted HEUs are to be weighed with a scale and the measured weights are to be compared to the total spreadsheet weights, compensating for any extraneous weights that may have been carried onboard the HEUs at the time of testing.

ii) Inclining Experiments. The completed HEUs are to be inclined on the surface and/or submerged (as applicable) in order to determine the GM (the distance between CGand the metacenter CM) and/or GB (the distance between CGand CB). Guidance for performing inclining experiments may be obtained from ASTM F 1321. Such testing is not required for HEUs that are duplicates (100% identical) of a previously tested HEU.

iii) Towable HEUs are to undergo a towing test at maximum speed in order to verify that there is no likelihood of the HEUs capsizing as a result of the towing force.

iv) HEUs designed for underwater operations are to undergo an unmanned test dive to its design depth in the presence of the Surveyor.

For this test dive, the HEU is to be loaded to its maximum weight, and is to be pressurized to its design depth using compressed air or breathing gases.

The test dive is to be conducted by increasing the submergence of the HEU in inc

to be pressurized to its design depth using compressed air or breathing gases.

The test dive is to be conducted by increasing the submergence of the HEU in increments until design depth is reached. Once at design depth, constant depth is to be maintained and functional testing of the HEU systems and equipment is to be carried-out as per the manufacturer's instructions. The test dive is to demonstrate satisfactory operation of the HEU systems and equipment.

Upon completion of the test dive, the following is to be carried-out on the surface:

a) Visual examination of the welded joints, penetrations, viewports and hatches of the HEU, as well as the HEU systems and equipment.

b) Functional testing of the HEU systems and equipment, as per the manufacturer's instructions.

The test dive may be a single dive as described above, or a series of dives to accomplish the same purpose. Where the depth of water available is less than the design depth, the test dive may be conducted to the rated depth (i.e., depth reached during the test dive). For such cases, both the rated depth, as well as the design depth of the HEU are to be indicated in the Record. The rated depth may be subsequently inc

t dive). For such cases, both the rated depth, as well as the design depth of the HEU are to be indicated in the Record. The rated depth may be subsequently increased by performing a test dive to a greater depth, not exceeding the design depth of the HEU, in the presence of the Surveyor.

11.5 HEU Launch Systems

11.5.1 (2017)

Each HEU is to be provided with a dedicated launch system that serves as the primary means for the safe and timely evacuation of the divers. HEU launch systems are to comply with the applicable requirements of Section 20. Alternatively, launch systems for lifeboat type HEUs (such as SPHLs) are to comply with the applicable requirements of IMO Resolution A.692(17) "Guidelines and Specifications for Hyperbaric Evacuation Systems" and IMO Resolutions MSC.48(66) and MSC.81(70) "Life-Saving Appliances Code", as well as any subsequent amendments to the same.

11.5.2

Launch systems are to be designed for safely launching the HEUs when the ship/offshore facility is under unfavorable conditions of up to 20 degrees list and 10 degrees trim either way in the fully loaded or unloaded condition.

11.5.3

Each launch system is to be provided with a suitable means that de

grees list and 10 degrees trim either way in the fully loaded or unloaded condition.

11.5.3

Each launch system is to be provided with a suitable means that depends on either stored mechanical power or gravity to safely launch the HEU and is independent of the power supply of the ship/offshore facility.

11.5.4 (2011)

For launch systems using falls for launching HEUs, the means provided for releasing the falls after the HEU is afloat is to permit easy disconnection. Falls are to be of rotation resistant and corrosion-resistant steel wire rope. The length of the falls is to be sufficient to allow the HEU to be launched in water when the ship/offshore facility is at its lightest draft and under unfavorable conditions of up to 20 degrees list and 10 degrees trim either way. (See 20/7 and 20/51).

11.7 HEU Recovery and Life Support Equipment

11.7.1 Recovery Equipment

11.7.1(a) Diving system operators are to ensure that suitable recovery equipment is available for recovering HEUs after they have been launched at sea. Alternatively, suitable equipment is to be available for towing the HEU to a shore-based reception facility or another vessel or the HEU is to be self-propelled. The eq

ively, suitable equipment is to be available for towing the HEU to a shore-based reception facility or another vessel or the HEU is to be self-propelled. The equipment is to be available at a suitable location from where it can reach the HEU within a reasonable amount of time. Diving system operators are to ensure that the recovery equipment is well maintained and ready for use at all times when diving operations are being carried out.

11.7.1(b) Recovery equipment, when part of a classed saturation diving system, is to meet the applicable requirements of these Rules.

11.7.1(c) Permanently marked clear instructions are to be provided adjacent to the recovery equipment. These instructions are to indicate the total in-air weight of the fully loaded HEU and the correct method for recovery.

11.7.2 Life Support Equipment

11.7.2(a) Diving system operators are to ensure that adequate life support equipment is available for maintaining appropriate life support conditions within HEUs after recovery or while it is being towed / self-propelled to a shore-based reception facility. This life support equipment may take the form of a portable life support package or fixed equipment onboard a v

f-propelled to a shore-based reception facility. This life support equipment may take the form of a portable life support package or fixed equipment onboard a vessel. The equipment is to be available at a suitable location from where it can reach the HEU within a reasonable amount of time. Diving system operators are to ensure that the life support equipment is well maintained and ready for use at all times when diving operations are being carried out.

11.7.2(b) Life support equipment, when part of a classed saturation diving system, is to meet the applicable requirements of these Rules.

11.7.2(c) The life support equipment is to be provided with an umbilical of suitable length to connect to the HEU. For HEUs that are being towed, the umbilical is to be longer than the towing line. The umbilical is to provide connections for breathing gases, hot/cold water, electrical power, communication, etc.

11.7.2(d) The life support equipment (including the breathing gases and connections) is to be compatible with the HEU.

11.7.2(e) The operational procedures for use of the life support equipment are to be available in the HEU and with the life support equipment.

13 Dive Control Stations

The operational procedures for use of the life support equipment are to be available in the HEU and with the life support equipment.

13 Dive Control Stations

13.1

Diving systems are to be provided with a centralized dive control station for the safe operation of the systems.

13.3

Dive control stations are to meet the applicable requirements of Section 21.

13.5

Dive control stations are to be provided with suitable equipment for recording all audio communications with divers in bells and in the water.

13.7 (2020)

Shipping containers used for housing portable dive control stations are to meet the applicable requirements of the ABS Rules for Certification of Cargo Containersor the ABS Guide for Certification of Offshore Containers, based on intended use. Special consideration will be given to containers that are designed and fabricated to other recognized industry standards that are not less effective than the Rules or Guide.

15 Utility Vans

15.1 (2020)

Shipping containers used for housing diving system machinery, tools, spares, etc., that are part of a classed diving system are to meet the applicable requirements of the ABS Rules for Certification of Cargo Containersor t

tools, spares, etc., that are part of a classed diving system are to meet the applicable requirements of the ABS Rules for Certification of Cargo Containersor the ABS Guide for Certification of Offshore Containers, based on intended use. Special consideration will be given to containers that are designed and fabricated to other recognized industry standards that are not less effective than the Rules or Guide.

17 Interface Between Diving Systems and Dive Support Vessels/ Offshore Facilities

17.1 Installation of Diving Systems

17.1.1

Diving systems are to be installed in safe areas on a dive support vessel/offshore facility. Safe areas are those areas that are outside hazardous zones 0, 1 and 2 as defined by the International Electrotechnical Commission's Publication No.79-10 and Chapter 6 of the IMO MODU code:

Zone 0: A zone in which an explosive gas-air mixture is continuously present or present for long periods.

Zone 1: A zone in which an explosive gas-air mixture is likely to occur in normal operation.

Zone 2: A zone in which an explosive gas-air mixture is not likely to occur, and if it occurs it will only exist for a short time.

Special consideration will be given on a

e in which an explosive gas-air mixture is not likely to occur, and if it occurs it will only exist for a short time.

Special consideration will be given on a case-by-case basis for diving systems that may be installed in Zone 2 hazardous zones.

17.1.2 Angles of Inclination (2012)

For diving systems installed on ships or ship-shaped offshore facilities, the PVHOs, machinery and systems essential for the safe operation of the diving system are to be designed for operation under the inclinations specified in 14/17.1.2 TABLE 2 below:

17.1.4

Diving systems and breathing gas storage facilities are not to be installed in machinery spaces that have machinery not associated with the diving system.

17.1.5

Diving systems are to be installed in spaces or locations that are adequately ventilated and provided with suitable lighting. Diving systems are not to be installed in the vicinity of exhausts/ ventilation outlets from machinery spaces and galleys.

17.1.6

When any part of the diving system is located on open decks or similar structures, particular attention is to be given to providing reasonable protection from the sea, icing or any damage which may result from other activities onboard the dive support vessel/offshore facility.

17.1.7

In order to minimize deflections and to obtain satisfactory transfer of heavy loads through the deck foundations, it is recommended that diving systems be installed in such a way that the underdeck supporting structure of the dive support vessel/offshore facility is in-line with the

ommended that diving systems be installed in such a way that the underdeck supporting structure of the dive support vessel/offshore facility is in-line with the deck foundations.

17.1.8

The diving system is to be located so that the diving operations are not affected by propellers, thrusters or anchors of the dive support vessel/offshore facility.

17.1.9 (2013)

Diving operations are to be conducted as far away as practicable from overboard discharge outlets/ suction inlets of the vessel/offshore facility, in order to keep the diving site free of suction, turbulence and discharge products, which may obscure visibility in the water or might cause skin infections or expose divers to harmful chemicals.

17.1.10 (2013)

Impressed current system anodes, when installed in the vicinity of diving systems, are to be capable of being switched off during diving operations.

17.1.11 (2013)

It is recommended that pressure vessels for human occupancy (PVHOs) be are arranged along the longitudinal (fore and aft) direction of the vessel/offshore facility in order to minimize the rolling effect on divers within the PVHOs.

17.1.12

Approval of diving systems is based on the pressures, loads, en

/offshore facility in order to minimize the rolling effect on divers within the PVHOs.

17.1.12

Approval of diving systems is based on the pressures, loads, environmental and operating conditions as specified by the diving system designers/operators. For diving systems, these factors may change after the diving system is demobilized from one dive support vessel/offshore facility, installed on another dive support vessel/offshore facility and mobilized for use. It is the responsibility of the diving system operator to advise ABS of changes to the pressures, loads, environmental and operating conditions due to the transfer of diving systems. The plans affected by the changes are to be updated and resubmitted to ABS for approval by the responsible party.

17.2 Breathing Gas Storage (2013)

Breathing gas cylinders for diving systems are to be stored on the open deck or in well ventilated enclosed spaces, and away from flammable substances and sources of ignition.

Where the breathing gas cylinders are stored on the open deck, they are to be provided with weather protection (particularly from heavy seas and heat) and are to be effectively protected from mechanical damage. Suitable drai

hey are to be provided with weather protection (particularly from heavy seas and heat) and are to be effectively protected from mechanical damage. Suitable drainage of the storage area is to be provided.

The boundaries between enclosed spaces housing the breathing gas cylinders and other enclosed spaces are to be gas tight. Access doors for these enclosed spaces are to open outwards. Suitable drainage of the enclosed spaces is to be provided.

The outlets of relief valves or bursting discs are to be piped-outside the enclosed spaces to the open deck and away from sources of ignition.

Each enclosed space is to be provided with a forced ventilation system capable of providing at least eight air changes per hour based on the gross volume of the space. The ventilation system is to be independent of ventilation systems of other spaces. The ventilation system air is to be drawn from a non-hazardous area. Ventilation system fans are to be of non-sparking construction.

Breathing gas mixtures containing more than 25% Oxygen by volume are to be treated and handled as pure Oxygen. Cylinders with these mixtures are to be stored on the open deck. They are not to be stored below the deck.

Wh

ume are to be treated and handled as pure Oxygen. Cylinders with these mixtures are to be stored on the open deck. They are not to be stored below the deck.

When breathing gas mixtures containing less than 18% Oxygen by volume is stored in enclosed spaces, at least two Oxygen analyzers with audio-visual alarms are to be provided. The Oxygen analyzers are to monitor low Oxygen levels. At least one analyzer is to monitor the upper areas of the enclosed space and at least one analyzer is to monitor the lower areas of the enclosed space. These enclosed spaces are to be thoroughly cleaned of hydrocarbons, fat, grease and other debris.

Where the diving support vessel/offshore facility is also used for fire fighting operations, means are to be provided to protect the Oxygen cylinders from heat that may radiate from the fire that is being extinguished.

17.3 Support Systems Provided by Dive Support Vessels/Offshore Facilities

17.3.1

Support systems are those systems that are provided by dive support vessels/offshore facilities and are not an integrated part of the diving system. These include among others:

Electrical systems (Power supplies, etc.)

Piping systems (Compressed air syste

nd are not an integrated part of the diving system. These include among others:

Electrical systems (Power supplies, etc.)

Piping systems (Compressed air systems, hydraulic systems, sewage systems, etc.)

Structural fire protection

External fire extinguishing, fire detection and alarm systems

Structural support (Means of fastening diving systems to the hull structure, deck foundations, under-deck supporting structure, etc.)

Position keeping and stability of dive support vessels/offshore facilities

17.3.2

For diving systems installed on ABS classed vessels/offshore facilities, these support systems are to be in accordance with the Marine Vessel Rules. Drawings, specifications, data and calculations are to be submitted to ABS for review and approval.

17.3.3

When ABS-classed diving systems are installed on non-ABS-classed vessels/offshore facilities, documentation that these systems have been reviewed and approved by the Society classing the dive support vessel/offshore facility is to be provided. Alternatively, the details may be provided to ABS for review.

17.5 Position Keeping and Stability of Dive Support Vessels/Offshore Facilities

17.5.1

Dive support vessels/offshore

ails may be provided to ABS for review.

17.5 Position Keeping and Stability of Dive Support Vessels/Offshore Facilities

17.5.1

Dive support vessels/offshore facilities are to be capable of maintaining their positions safely during diving operations. The means to maintain position may be a mooring system with anchors or a dynamic positioning system.

17.5.2

Dynamic positioning systems used to maintain the position during diving operations are as a minimum to comply with the requirements for vessels with DPS-2 notation (Equipment Class 2 as per IMO MSC/Circ.645) or equivalent.

17.5.3

The dive control station and the control station for the dynamic positioning system are to be linked by a communication system and a manually operated alarm system.

17.7 Deck Foundations, Fastening Arrangements, and Vessel/Offshore Facility Structures (2013)

17.7.1 General Requirements

Diving systems are to be securely attached to the hull structure of dive support vessels/offshore facilities using suitable permanent means of fastening such as welding or bolting. (Lashing is not considered to be a permanent means of fastening).

The design of deck foundations and fastening arrangements are to co

ch as welding or bolting. (Lashing is not considered to be a permanent means of fastening).

The design of deck foundations and fastening arrangements are to consider the relative movement between the components of the diving system. In addition, the deck foundations and fastening arrangements are to be designed to meet any required survival conditions of the dive support vessel/offshore facility as may be specified by the Flag Administration.

In order to avoid exposing the diving system components to excessive deflections, the maximum deflections of the deck structure of dive support vessels/offshore facilities in-way of diving systems are to be evaluated. The evaluated deflections are not to exceed the defined acceptable level of deflections for the diving system.

17.7.2 Design Loads

Deck foundations, fastening arrangements and vessel/offshore facility structures in-way of diving systems are to be designed for the following loads:

17.7.2(a) Acceleration Loads.

Accelerations due to dynamic motion of the vessel/offshore facility are to be derived from recognized national or international standards, seakeeping analysis using recognized engineering methodology or from actual or

facility are to be derived from recognized national or international standards, seakeeping analysis using recognized engineering methodology or from actual or scaled-model tests, considering the most serve environmental conditions that the vessel/offshore facility is expected to encounter.

For offshore support vessels, the acceleration loads are to be derived in accordance with 5D-5-3/5.3.1 of the ABS Rules for Building and Classing Marine Vessels (MVR).

17.7.2(b) Static Inclination Loads.

Deck foundations and fastening arrangements for diving system components are to be designed for a static inclination of 30°.

17.7.2(c) Hydrostatic Testing Loads.

When it is intended to carry out hydrostatic testing of PVHOs and other pressure vessels while they are installed on the dive support vessel/offshore facility, the deck foundations and fastening arrangements are to be designed for supporting the maximum static load due to the hydrostatic testing, without unacceptable deflections.

17.7.2(d) Mating Loads.

When mating operations are to be carried out on the dive support vessel/offshore facility, the deck foundations and fastening arrangements of pressure vessels for human occupancy

ons are to be carried out on the dive support vessel/offshore facility, the deck foundations and fastening arrangements of pressure vessels for human occupancy (PVHO) are to be designed for mating loads. The mating loads are to consider a force of not less than twice the weight of the mating PVHO (diving bell, hyperbaric evacuation unit, etc.) and are to include other applicable loads such as the weight of entrapped mud and water.

17.7.2(e) Handling System Loads.

The maximum expected operational loads are to be considered for the design of deck foundations, fastening arrangements and vessel structures in way of handling systems (LARS, davits, cranes, etc.) and handling system elements (winches, sheaves, dampers, etc.).

When handling systems are used for handling manned objects such as Diving Bells, Diving Baskets, etc., a dynamic factor of at least 2g vertical, 1g transverse and 1g longitudinal is to be applied. When handling systems are used for handing unmanned objects, a dynamic factor of at least 1.75g vertical, 0.75g transverse and 0.75g longitudinal is to be applied. For permanently installed systems, consideration may be given to lower dynamic factors, when it can be show

transverse and 0.75g longitudinal is to be applied. For permanently installed systems, consideration may be given to lower dynamic factors, when it can be shown that the maximum expected loads are less than those specified above (See 20/7.3.4).

17.7.3 Allowable Stresses

The allowable stresses for the design are to be in accordance with the applicable requirements of the Rules/Guides used to Class the vessel/offshore structure.

For offshore support vessels, the allowable stresses are to be in accordance with 5D-5-3/5.3.6 of the ABS Rules for Building and Classing Marine Vessels (MVR).

17.9 Moon Pools (2013)

Moon pools, where provided for diving operations are to meet the following requirements:

Openings are to be a suitable distance from the deck edge, from cargo hatch covers, from superstructure breaks and from other area of structural discontinuity.

The side structure of moon pools are to be designed for impact loads from diving equipment that may be guided through the moon pools. The moon pool side structure is to comply with requirements for side shell plating given in the Rules/Guides used to Class the vessel/offshore structure. For offshore support vessels, the moon po

comply with requirements for side shell plating given in the Rules/Guides used to Class the vessel/offshore structure. For offshore support vessels, the moon pool side structure is to comply with requirements for side shell plating given in Section 3-2-2 of the ABS Rules for Building and Classing Marine Vessels (MVR).

The corner radius of the moon pool opening is not be less than 0.125 times the width of the moon-pool opening but it need not exceed 600 mm (24 in.). Free edges of the moon pool opening are to be suitably rounded in order to protect diving system umbilicals from sharp edges.

Means are to be provided to prevent personnel from falling into the moon pool.

17.11 Piping Systems (2013)

Piping systems carrying breathing gases under high pressure are not to be arranged inside accommodation spaces, engine rooms or similar compartments.

Piping systems carrying breathing gases are to run as far as practicable apart from electrical cable conduits.

Piping systems containing flammable fluids are not to run through enclosed spaces or open deck areas housing breathing gases.

17.13 Fire Protection

17.13.1 Diving Systems Installed in Enclosed Spaces

17.13.1(a) Enclosed spaces

osed spaces or open deck areas housing breathing gases.

17.13 Fire Protection

17.13.1 Diving Systems Installed in Enclosed Spaces

17.13.1(a) Enclosed spaces on dive support vessels/offshore facilities housing diving systems are to be separated from adjacent spaces by means of A-60 class bulkheads or decks. Piping and cables essential for the operation of the diving system are to be laid in separate structural ducts insulated to the A-60 class standard.

17.13.1(b) Enclosed spaces are to be provided with a separate forced ventilation system capable of providing at least eight air changes per hour. The air is to be drawn from a non-hazardous area.

17.13.2 Diving Systems Installed on Open Decks

When diving systems are installed on open decks or similar structures that are directly adjacent to Category A machinery spaces, the systems are to be separated from the machinery spaces by A-60 class bulkheads or decks.

17.15 Fire Fighting

17.15.1 External Fire Extinguishing Systems

17.15.1(a) Diving Systems Installed in Enclosed Spaces (2013)

system is selected, the complete discharge of the extinguishing system in the enclosed space is not to result in a toxic concentration. Extingu

d Spaces (2013)

system is selected, the complete discharge of the extinguishing system in the enclosed space is not to result in a toxic concentration. Extinguishing agents/propellants that are carcinogenic, mutagenic, or teratogenic at the expected concentrations during use are not permitted. Agents/propellants are not to be used in concentrations greater than the cardiac sensitization NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level) and the ALC (Approximate Lethal Concentration) per IMO MSC/Circ.776.

ii) Means are to be provided for cooling the windows of PVHOs installed in enclosed spaces.

iii) Portable fire extinguishers of approved types are to be distributed throughout the enclosed space containing the diving system. One of the portable fire-extinguishers is to be stowed near the entrance to the enclosed space. Spare charges are to be provided on board for 100% of the first ten extinguishers and 50% of the remaining extinguishers installed within the enclosed space.

iv) Enclosed spaces intended for storage of breathing gas cylinders/pressure vessels are to be fitted with a manually actuated fixed water spray system with an application rate of at least 10 litres/m2 per minute of t

cylinders/pressure vessels are to be fitted with a manually actuated fixed water spray system with an application rate of at least 10 litres/m2 per minute of the horizontal projected area, in order to cool and protect such cylinders/pressure vessels during fires. Alternatively, the enclosed spaces may be fitted with a water-mist system with an application rate of not less than 5 liters/m2 per minute.

17.15.1(b) Diving Systems Installed on Open Decks

i) The areas where diving systems are situated on decks or similar structures of dive support vessels/offshore facilities, are to be equipped with fire extinguishing systems suitable for the locations and areas concerned. Fire hoses connected to the fire main of the dive support vessels/offshore facilities may be considered as providing the necessary protection.

ii) Means are to be provided for cooling the windows of PVHOs installed on open decks or similar structures.

17.15.2 External Fire Detection and Alarm Systems

17.15.2(a) Diving Systems Installed in Enclosed Spaces (2013).

Enclosed spaces on dive support vessels/ offshore facilities are to be provided with automatic fire detection and alarm systems suitable for the locatio

2013).

Enclosed spaces on dive support vessels/ offshore facilities are to be provided with automatic fire detection and alarm systems suitable for the location and area concerned. For offshore support vessels, the fire detection and alarm systems are to comply with the applicable requirements of 4-7-3/11 of the ABS Rules for Building and Classing Marine Vessels (MVR).

The loop or group of detectors covering these enclosed spaces is to be independent of those for other spaces.

The fire detection panel is to be located in the vessel's position control station and is to be provided with repeaters at the dive control station and the engine control room.

17.15.2(b) Diving Systems Installed on Open Decks.

The open decks or similar structures of dive support vessels/offshore facilities are to be provided with automatic fire detection and alarm systems suitable for the location and area concerned. Consideration will be given to continuous direct visual/video camera monitoring of the diving system by the dive control station personnel in lieu of fire detection and alarm systems.

S E C T I O N 1 5

Hyperbaric Facilities (2021)

1 Scope

This Section along with Sections 1 through 10 provide the requirements for the design, fabrication and testing of hyperbaric facilities for marine applications, including Hyperbaric Diving Simulators and Hyperbaric Reception Facilities.

The scope of this section does not include the following types of facilities:

Hyperbaric facilities intended to be used in a clinical setting for medical hyperbaric therapy (e.g., Medical Hyperbaric Chambers)

Hypobaric facilities for high altitude training (e.g., Altitude Chambers)

See Section 2 for the definition of the applicable terms.

2 Class Notations

New construction hyperbaric facilities that are built under ABS survey and meet the requirements of Subsections 15/3 and 15/4 below are to be assigned the class notation ✠ A1 Hyperbaric Facility, subject to the conditions of classification referenced in Section 1 of these Rules. New and existing hyperbaric reception facilities that are not built under ABS survey and meet the requirements of Subsections 15/3 and 15/4 below are to be assigned the class notation A1 Hyperbaric Facility

es that are not built under ABS survey and meet the requirements of Subsections 15/3 and 15/4 below are to be assigned the class notation A1 Hyperbaric Facility, subject to the conditions of classification referenced in Section 1 of these Rules.

New construction hyperbaric diving simulators that are built under ABS survey and meet the requirements of Subsections 15/3, 15/4 and 15/5 below are to be assigned the class notation ✠ A1 Hyperbaric Diving Simulator, subject to the conditions of classification referenced in Section 1 of these Rules. New and existing hyberbaric diving simulators that are not built under ABS survey and meet the requirements of Subsections 15/3, 15/4 and 15/5 below are to be assigned the class notation A1 Hyperbaric Diving Simulator, subject to the conditions of classification referenced in Section 1 of these Rules.

New construction hyperbaric reception facilities that are built under ABS survey and meet the requirements of Subsections 15/3, 15/4 and 15/6 below are to be assigned the class notation ✠ A1 Hyperbaric Reception Facility, subject to the conditions of classification referenced in Section 1 of these Rules. New and existing hyperbaric reception faci

A1 Hyperbaric Reception Facility, subject to the conditions of classification referenced in Section 1 of these Rules. New and existing hyperbaric reception facilities that are not built under ABS survey and meet the requirements of Subsections 15/3, 15/4 and 15/6 below are to be assigned the class notation A1 Hyperbaric Reception Facility, subject to the conditions of classification referenced in Section 1 of these Rules.

3 General Requirements

3.1 Pressure Vessels for Human Occupancy (PVHOs)

3.1.1 Metallic Pressure Boundaries

Metallic pressure boundaries are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 4, 5, and 6.

3.1.2 Windows and Viewports

Windows and viewports are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Section 7.

Windows are to be provided with appropriate means of protection to prevent mechanical damage.

3.1.3 Personnel Access Hatches/Manways

3.1.3(a)

Metallic hatches are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 4, 5, and 6. The design of hatches (including the doors, hatch operating and hinge mechanisms) are to take into account all applicable loads.

3.1.3(b)

Hatches that are used as a normal means of p

ncluding the doors, hatch operating and hinge mechanisms) are to take into account all applicable loads.

3.1.3(b)

Hatches that are used as a normal means of personnel ingress or egress are to have an opening diameter of not less than 610 mm (24 in.). When hyperbaric facilities are used for diver training or recovery, the hatch opening diameter is not to be less than 711 mm (28 in.) in order to facilitate the recovery of an unconscious diver wearing full diving gear. Larger opening diameters may be necessary to accommodate divers with emergency life support systems activated.

3.1.3(c)

Hatches are to be operable from both internal and external sides. The means for opening and closing of hatches is to permit operation by a single person under all anticipated operating and emergency conditions. Locking mechanisms, when provided, are to be operable from both sides of a closed hatch. Means are to be provided for equalizing the pressure on both sides of hatches prior to opening.

3.1.3(d)

In general, hatches are to be designed to seat with pressure. Hatches that unseat with pressure will be given special consideration based on the application of the PVHO. Hatches that unseat with pre

to seat with pressure. Hatches that unseat with pressure will be given special consideration based on the application of the PVHO. Hatches that unseat with pressure are to be provided with locking mechanisms and safety interlocks. Interlocks are to prevent accidental opening of the doors while under pressure, and are to prevent pressurization if not fully engaged. Locking mechanisms and interlocks are to be designed such that the fully closed position of the mechanisms are clearly verifiable before application of pressure.

3.1.3(e)

Hatch coaming ways are to be free from obstacles that would prevent personnel ingress or egress.

3.1.3(f)

Hatches are to be provided with a mechanical means for securing in the fully open position.

3.1.3(g)

Means are to be provided to prevent inadvertent operation of hatches, including doors, which use springs or similar assist mechanisms. Fluids used in hatch assist mechanisms are to be compatible with the internal environment of the PVHO.

3.1.4 Nonmetallic Materials

Paints, coatings, insulation and other internal and external materials are to meet the applicable requirements of Subsection 3/1.

3.1.5 Nameplates

PVHOs are to be fitted with per

sulation and other internal and external materials are to meet the applicable requirements of Subsection 3/1.

3.1.5 Nameplates

PVHOs are to be fitted with permanent nameplates indicating the maximum allowable internal and external working pressures, maximum and minimum rated temperatures, internal and external hydrostatic test pressures, manufacturer's name, serial number and year of fabrication. This information is to be also stamped on a rim of a flange of the PVHO. The nameplates are to be stainless steel or other suitable material and are to be permanently attached.

3.2 External Structures

External structures include all structural members located outside the pressure boundaries of PVHOs. These include members such as support frames, skids and lifting lugs.

The design of each welded connection is to facilitate appropriate inspection. All welds on external welded connections are to be sealed or full penetration welds.

3.2.1 Support Frames / Skids

3.2.1(a)

Support frames/skids of PVHOs are to be of construction adequate to the function performed, consideration being given to their size and the anticipated loads which may be imposed on them, and they are to be constructed

quate to the function performed, consideration being given to their size and the anticipated loads which may be imposed on them, and they are to be constructed following sound engineering practice in accordance with approved plans.

3.2.1(b)

Loads to be considered include applicable loads such as the weight of the fully loaded PVHO (including equipment, supplies and people). Where mating operations are carried-out, the mating loads are to consider a force of not less than twice the weight of the mating PVHO (e.g., Hyperbaric Evacuation Unit). When it is intended to carry out hydrostatic testing of the PVHO with the support frames/skids installed, then the maximum static load due to the hydrostatic testing is to also be considered in the design. Calculated stresses are not to exceed the allowable stresses fa as obtained from the following equation:

3.2.1(c)

Support frames/skids of PVHOs that also support handling system elements such as A-frames and winches are to meet the applicable requirements of Section 20, "Handling Systems".

3.2.2 Lifting Lugs / Attachments

Lifting lugs/attachments of PVHOs are to be suitable for handling the PVHOs during installation/demobilization. Calculated stresses are not to exceed 1/3 of the minimum material yield strength or 1/5 of the minimum material tensile strength, whichever is less.

3.3 Piping Systems

i) Piping systems are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 8 and 9.

ii) PVHOs are to be equipped with valves, gauges and other fittings as necessary to control and monitor the internal environments of compartments from the control station of the hyperbaric facility. All interior breathing and pressure supply controls are to be provided with means of overriding and controlling them from the exterior.

iii) A manually operated stop valve is to be provided at each PVHO pressure boundary piping penetration. Additionally, piping exclusively carrying fluids in

r.

iii) A manually operated stop valve is to be provided at each PVHO pressure boundary piping penetration. Additionally, piping exclusively carrying fluids into the PVHO are to have a check valve close to and downstream of the stop valve.

iv) Each compartment of a PVHO which may be pressurized separately is to be fitted with a pressure relief device. Rupture discs are not to be used. A quick-operating manual shut-off valve is to be installed between the PVHO pressure boundary and the pressure relief device and is to be wired opened with a frangible wire. Pressure relief devices are to be capable of preventing the internal pressure from rising more than 10% above the maximum allowable working pressure of the PVHO.

v) Piping systems are to be designed so as to minimize the noise inside the PVHOs during normal operations. Mufflers / silencers are to be provided on blowdown and exhaust outlets.

vi) Anti-suction protective devices are to be provided inside PVHOs on the openings of exhaust lines.

vii) All high-pressure piping is to be well-protected and well-supported against mechanical damage.

viii) Piping systems carrying mixed gas or oxygen under high pressure are not to be arr

g is to be well-protected and well-supported against mechanical damage.

viii) Piping systems carrying mixed gas or oxygen under high pressure are not to be arranged inside accommodation spaces, machinery rooms or similar compartments.

ix) Flexible hoses, except for umbilicals, are to be reduced to a minimum.

3.4 Life Support and Environmental Control Systems

3.4.1 General

i) Life support systems are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 8 and 9.

ii) Hyperbaric facilities are to be provided with the necessary equipment to generate, monitor and maintain suitable life support conditions inside the various PVHOs and transfer trunks/tunnels. Life support systems are to be such that adequate quantities of gases for operation with the maximum number of occupants, and at the maximum pressure for normal and emergency conditions are provided. A sufficient supply of gases essential for the desaturation (or decompression) period in accordance with the applicable decompression table is to be kept available for the expected maximum number of occupants. In determining the duration and amount of life support necessary, consideration is to be given to the environmen

e for the expected maximum number of occupants. In determining the duration and amount of life support necessary, consideration is to be given to the environmental conditions, the Oxygen (O3) and mixed gas consumption and Carbon Dioxide (CO2) generation under such conditions, the heat input or removal and the emergency services that may be available for the decompression of occupants. Gas losses as a result of using toilet facilities which discharge outside the PVHOs and medical/equipment lock operations are to be taken into consideration in determining the amount of gases and Carbon Dioxide (CO3) absorbent required for the hyperbaric operation.

iii) The normal life support system is to be designed so that the partial pressure of Oxygen (O2) within PVHOs are kept within the appropriate limits for the particular application (mixed gas diving, saturation diving, etc.) as specified in the US Navy Diving Manual or an equivalent recognized national or international standard. Carbon Dioxide (CO2) removal systems are to be designed so that the partial pressure of Carbon Dioxide (CO2) within PVHOs do not exceed 0.005 atmosphere absolute (ata).

Life Support Capacity (Normal and Reserve).

Life Support Capacity (Normal and Reserve). At the commencement of operations, the normal breathing gas supply and Carbon Dioxide (CO2) removal systems of the hyperbaric facility are to have sufficient capacity for the anticipated mission time plus a reserve capacity that is not less than 48 hours.

v) Each compartment of the PVHO is to be provided with two (2) independent gas pressurization systems.

3.4.2 Breathing Gas Systems

3.4.2(a)

Each compartment of the PVHO is to be provided with two (2) independent breathing gas supply systems.

3.4.2(b)

For decompression / recompression applications, each applicable compartment of the PVHO is to be provided with the following:

i) One (1) gas exhaust system

ii) One (1) Built-in Breathing System (BIBS) with breathing masks (See 3.4.2(i) below)

3.4.2(c)

Where pure Oxygen $(O_2)$ or gases containing more than 25% Oxygen $(O_2)$ by volume is being supplied, a separate piping system is to be provided for this purpose.

3.4.2(d)

The design pressurization and exhaust depressurization rates for hyperbaric operations are to b

ate piping system is to be provided for this purpose.

3.4.2(d)

The design pressurization and exhaust depressurization rates for hyperbaric operations are to be determined by the designer and are to be consistent with the rates specified in the US Navy Diving Manual or equivalent recognized national or international standards. For treatment recompression applications, the gas supply system to the chambers is to be designed for a minimum pressurization rate of 60 FSW/minute up to 60 FSW followed by a rate of 30 FSW/minute thereafter.

3.4.2(e)

The gas supply and exhaust systems are to be arranged to provide even mixing of the gases.

3.4.2(f)

Gas system valves are to be arranged so that valve leakage does not lead to inadvertent mixing of pure Oxygen (O2) or gases containing more than 25% Oxygen (O2) by volume with other breathing gases. Intersections between Oxygen (O2) and non-Oxygen (O2) systems are to be isolated by two shut-off valves with a vent valve in between.

3.4.2(g)

Filters and automatic pressure regulators are to be arranged so that they can be isolated without interrupting vital gas supplies.

3.4.2(h)

The discharge from relief devices, vents and exhausts are to

re to be arranged so that they can be isolated without interrupting vital gas supplies.

3.4.2(h)

The discharge from relief devices, vents and exhausts are to be vented away from sources of ignition, personnel or any area where the presence of the discharged gases could be hazardous.

3.4.2(i) Built-in Breathing Systems (BIBS)

i) The BIBS is to supply emergency/treatment gas through a dedicated piping system to breathing masks (either full-face masks or oral-nasal masks). Breathing masks are to be suitable for the intended application and are to be permanently connected or easily connectable to the BIBS supply piping.

ii) Compartments that serve as living facilities are to be provided with BIBS masks corresponding to the maximum number of occupants for which the compartment is rated plus one. Compartments that serve as transfer locks are to be provided with BIBS masks corresponding to the maximum number of occupants that may simultaneously transfer through the lock plus one. All other compartments are to be provided with at least two BIBS masks.

iii) The BIBS supply piping is to be independent of the normal gas supply piping to the compartment.

iv) The emergency and treatment

at least two BIBS masks.

iii) The BIBS supply piping is to be independent of the normal gas supply piping to the compartment.

iv) The emergency and treatment gas cylinders are not to be used for supplying the normal gas supply system of the compartment.

v) The capacity of the emergency/treatment gas supplies is to be sufficient for the safe conclusion of the hyperbaric facility's operation.

vi) The system is to be designed such that the partial pressure of Carbon Dioxide (CO2 ) in the gas being inhaled does not to exceed 0.015 atmosphere absolute (ata).

vii) The BIBS is to be capable of supplying emergency gas at the maximum operating depth and treatment gas at the appropriate partial pressures as per the appropriate treatment table.

viii) BIBS exhaust systems are to be provided with a back-pressure regulator and a secondary means to prevent pressure differential to the diver using the mask, if the back pressure regulator were to fail.

ix) Automatic pressure reducers are to be provided on supply lines. Alternative arrangements that provide an equivalent level of safety will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

x) Where open circuit systems are used, the effects of change

angements that provide an equivalent level of safety will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

x) Where open circuit systems are used, the effects of change in the compartment pressure and oxygen concentration are to be considered.

xi) The exhaust sides of masks intended for oxygen service are to be connected to an external dump or the BIBS is to be of closed-circuit type. When closed-circuit type systems are used, consideration is to be given to the change in oxygen concentration resulting from operational procedures.

3.4.3 Carbon Dioxide (CO2 ) Removal Systems

i) Each compartment of the PVHO is to be provided with means for Carbon Dioxide (CO2 ) removal.

ii) Carbon Dioxide (CO2 ) removal systems used for normal operation are to be provided with full redundancy to maintain the CO2 concentration within acceptable limits.

3.4.4 Emergency Life Support System

i) In addition to the normal breathing gas and Carbon Dioxide (CO2 ) removal systems, an emergency life support system is to be provided. The emergency life support system is to be independent of the normal life support system.

ii) Emergency breathing gas is to be supplied to either full-face masks, oral-nasal masks or

rt system is to be independent of the normal life support system.

ii) Emergency breathing gas is to be supplied to either full-face masks, oral-nasal masks or self-contained re-breathers suitable for the intended service. One mask per occupant of the PVHO is to be provided.

iv) The emergency life support system is to be designed so that the partial pressure of Carbon Dioxide (CO2 ) in the gas being inhaled does not exceed 0.015 atmosphere absolute (ata).

v) The capacity of the emergency life support system is to be sufficient to permit evacuation of the compartment during events such as a contaminated atmosphere, as per the emergency operating procedures of the hyperbaric facility.

3.4.5 External Environmental Control Units (ECUs)

i) External Environmental Control Units (ECUs), when fitted, are to be capable of providing heating/cooling, humidity control and Carbon Dioxide (CO2 ) removal services.

ii) The piping between the ECU and the PVHO is to be appropriately sized to ensure adequate circulation of gases between the ECU and the PVHO so as to maintain the life support parameters within the allowable limits.

iii) The piping at the PVHO pressure boundary penetration is to b

e ECU and the PVHO so as to maintain the life support parameters within the allowable limits.

iii) The piping at the PVHO pressure boundary penetration is to be provided with a nonreturn valve on the supply line and a flow-fuse on the exhaust line. These may be fitted either internally or externally.

iv) Sizing calculations for flow-fuses are to be submitted for review. In addition, means are to be considered for resetting flow-fuses.

3.5 Electrical Systems and Equipment

3.5.1 General

i) Electrical systems and equipment are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Section 10.

ii) Electrical systems and equipment installed within PVHOs are to be limited to those necessary for the safe operation of the PVHO and the monitoring of its occupants.

iii) Electrical systems and equipment, including power supply arrangements, are to be designed for the environment in which they will operate to minimize the risk of fires, explosions, electrical shocks, emission of toxic gases and galvanic action on the PVHO.

iv) The electrical systems and equipment within the PVHO are to be suitable for hyperbaric applications and are to be rated for the maximum anticipated pressure wit

The electrical systems and equipment within the PVHO are to be suitable for hyperbaric applications and are to be rated for the maximum anticipated pressure within the PVHO. These systems and equipment are to be such that repeated pressurization and depressurization will not cause damage.

3.5.2 Normal Power Supply

At the commencement of each operation, the normal power supply of the hyperbaric facility is to have sufficient capacity for the anticipated mission time plus a reserve capacity that is not less than 48 hours.

3.5.3 Emergency Power Supply

i) In the event of failure of the normal source of electrical power to the hyperbaric facility, an independent emergency source of electrical power is to be available. The emergency source of power is to have sufficient capacity for 150% of the time required for safe termination of operations of the hyperbaric facility, while supplying the emergency equipment (such as life support equipment, communication equipment and internal lighting).

ii) The emergency source of electrical power is to be located as far apart as practicable from the normal source of electrical power.

3.5.4 Generators

Generators that are part of the classed hype

l power is to be located as far apart as practicable from the normal source of electrical power.

3.5.4 Generators

Generators that are part of the classed hyperbaric facility, and are used for supplying normal or emergency power, are to comply with the following:

i) Generators of 100 kW (135 hp) and over, are to be certified for compliance with the appropriate provisions of Section 4-8-3 of the Marine Vessel Rules.

ii) Generators of less than 100 kW (135 hp), are to be designed, constructed and tested in accordance with established industrial practices and manufacturer's specifications. Manufacturer's tests for generators less than 100 kW (135 hp) are to include at least the tests described in 4-8-3/3.15.2 through 4-8-3/3.15.11 of the Marine Vessel Rules, regardless of the standard of construction. The test certificates are to be made available when requested by the Surveyor. Acceptance of these generators will be based on satisfactory performance tests after installation.

3.6 Communication Systems and Equipment

3.6.1

Hyperbaric facilities are to be provided with voice communication systems and equipment. These communication systems and equipment are to be arranged for direct

Hyperbaric facilities are to be provided with voice communication systems and equipment. These communication systems and equipment are to be arranged for direct two-way communication between the control station of the hyperbaric facility and the following as applicable:

Each compartment of the hyperbaric facility's PVHOs

Where mating operations are carried-out, the unit (e.g., Hyperbaric Evacuation Unit) that is mating with the hyperbaric facility's PVHO

3.6.2

The control station is to have the capability to initiate communication without action from the occupants.

3.6.3

i) Communication systems are to consist of a primary and secondary system.

ii) The primary system is to be supplied by two independent sources of power, one of which is to be the emergency source of power. Speech unscramblers are to be provided when mixed gas is used.

iii) The secondary system is to be fully independent of the primary system. It may consist of a sound-powered telephone or other means that are capable of operating in the event of main and emergency power failure.

3.7 Fire Fighting and Fire Detection

3.7.1 Internal Fire Extinguishing Systems for PVHOs

3.7.1(a) General

i) Each compartment of a PVHO is to be provided with suitable means for fire fighting. This may include fixed fire extinguishing systems or portable hyperbaric fire extinguishers.

ii) The means of fire fighting are to be suitable for operation at all internal pressures, up to the maximum operating pressure of the PVHO.

iii) Chemical extinguishing agents/propellants with toxic or narcotic effects for any operating conditions are not permitted. Salt water is not to be used as an extinguishing agent/ propellant. Choking hazards of extinguishing agents/propellants are to be taken into consideration. Extinguishing agents/propellants that are carcinogenic, mutagenic, or teratogenic at the expected concentrations during use are not permitted.

iv) The capacity of extinguishing systems using gaseous propellants is to be such that complete discharge of the extinguishing system will not result in a toxic concentration.

v) Consideration is to be given to the increase in compartment pressure resulting from the discharge of the extinguishing agents/prope

n a toxic concentration.

v) Consideration is to be given to the increase in compartment pressure resulting from the discharge of the extinguishing agents/propellants.

3.7.1(b) Fixed Fire Extinguishing Systems

i) Fixed fire extinguishing systems are to be capable of providing rapid and efficient distribution of the extinguishing agent to any part of the compartment under all foreseeable conditions.

ii) Fixed fire extinguishing systems are to be suitable for operation at all internal pressures up to the maximum operating pressure of the PVHO.

iii) Means are to be provided to actuate fixed fire extinguishing systems from within and outside the PVHOs. Suitable safeguards are to be provided to prevent inadvertent actuation.

iv) The extinguishing agent is to be fresh water or other liquid agents suitable for use in manned compartments.

v) Fixed fire extinguishing systems are to be capable of recharging without the need for depressurizing the PVHOs.

vi) Fixed fire extinguishing systems are to have the capability to stop and reactivate their discharge from within and outside PVHOs.

vii) Fixed extinguishing systems are to be functionally tested in the presence of the Surveyor over

reactivate their discharge from within and outside PVHOs.

vii) Fixed extinguishing systems are to be functionally tested in the presence of the Surveyor over the full range of internal pressures, until the maximum operating pressure of the PVHO.

3.7.1(c) Portable Hyperbaric Fire Extinguishers

i) When PVHOs are not provided with fixed fire extinguishing systems, the fire extinguishing means is to consist of portable hyperbaric fire extinguishers.

ii) Hyperbaric fire extinguishers are to be suitable for operation at all internal pressures up to the maximum operating pressure of the PVHO.

iii) Hyperbaric fire extinguishers are to be of an approved type. The extinguishers are to be provided with documentation confirming their approval by an appropriate third-party independent agency or laboratory for hyperbaric services.

iv) The extinguishing agent is to be distilled water or other non-conductive liquid agents suitable for use in manned compartments.

3.7.2 Internal Fire Detection and Alarm Systems for PVHOs

i) Each compartment of the PVHO is to be provided with automatic fire detection and alarm systems.

ii) The fire detection and alarm systems are to be suitable for operatio

artment of the PVHO is to be provided with automatic fire detection and alarm systems.

ii) The fire detection and alarm systems are to be suitable for operation under hyperbaric conditions. Where such systems are unsuitable, consideration will be given to alternate arrangements.

iii) Faults (voltage failure, breakage in the detection loops, earth faults, etc.) in the fire detection and alarm systems are to be indicated at the control station of the hyperbaric facility by means of visual and audible signals.

3.8 PVHO Layout and Outfitting

3.8.1 General

i) Hyperbaric facilities are to be provided with at least one chamber with two separate compartments or two separate interconnected chambers so designed as to permit ingress or egress of personnel while one compartment or chamber remains pressurized.

ii) Each compartment of the PVHO is to be provided with means of visual monitoring of the internal occupants from the outside. At least one viewport is to be provided for each compartment.

iii) PVHOs are to be provided with adequate means of normal and emergency lighting of sufficient luminosity to allow the occupants to read gauges and operate essential systems within each compart

adequate means of normal and emergency lighting of sufficient luminosity to allow the occupants to read gauges and operate essential systems within each compartment safely. A general interior illumination level of at least 270 lumens/m2 (25 foot-candles) is to be provided, with 540 lumens/m2 (50 foot-candles) provided over bunks and in work areas under normal operating conditions.

iv) PVHOs are to be provided with suitable means of heating, cooling and controlling humidity, to maintain a comfortable temperature and humidity level for the occupants. The heating, cooling and humidity control systems are to have full redundancy.

v) PVHOs or PVHO compartments that serve as living facilities and other compartments that may be used for decompression are to be provided with suitable medical/equipment locks. See Subsection 15/3.9.2 below.

vi) All compartments of PVHOs are to be provided with gas circulation systems. The circulation systems are to have the capability to maintain homogenous gas mixtures and avoid stratification of gas layers within the compartments.

3.8.2 Continuously Occupied PVHOs

When PVHOs are continuously occupied for 12 hours or longer, they are to be provided wit

gas layers within the compartments.

3.8.2 Continuously Occupied PVHOs

When PVHOs are continuously occupied for 12 hours or longer, they are to be provided with the following features:

3.8.2(a)

PVHOs that serve as living facilities are to be provided with one bunk for each occupant. The dimensions of the bunks are to be suitable for enabling the occupants to rest comfortably.

3.8.2(b)

The viewports/windows for PVHOs that serve as living facilities are to allow the bunks to be seen as much as possible over their entire lengths from the outside.

3.8.2(c)

PVHOs that serve as living facilities are to have internal dimensions sufficient to allow most occupants to stand upright and to lie down and stretch out comfortably on their bunks.

3.8.2(d)

PVHOs or PVHO compartments that serve as transfer locks are to have internal dimensions sufficient to allow the simultaneous transfer of at least two persons. The floodable volume is not to be less than 105 ft3 (3.0 m3 ).

3.8.2(e)

Toilet, hand washing, and shower arrangements are to be provided and are to be installed outside the living facilities in separate compartments. These arrangements are to comply with the following:

i) Flush

e to be provided and are to be installed outside the living facilities in separate compartments. These arrangements are to comply with the following:

i) Flush Type Toilets

Flush-type toilets, when installed, are to be provided with the following:

i) An interlock to prevent flushing of the toilet while the user is seated.

ii) Stand-offs on the toilet seat to prevent the formation of a complete seal between the toilet bowl and the user.

iii) Water for flushing at a safe and sufficient pressure above the internal pressure of the PVHO at the time of flushing.

iv) Flushing systems are to require two positive manual actions for operation.

v) Flushing systems are to have means to limit the volume of gas exhausted with each flushing operation.

vii) Means for communicating with the control station are to be provided in the vicinity of flush type toilets.

ii) Hand Washing and Shower Systems

Hand washing and shower systems are to be provided with the following:

i) A sink for hand washing and a shower head for showering.

ii) Water at a safe and sufficient pressure above the internal pressure of the PVHO at the time of washing or showering.

iii) Effluent drain from the hand washing

Water at a safe and sufficient pressure above the internal pressure of the PVHO at the time of washing or showering.

iii) Effluent drain from the hand washing and shower systems is to be routed to an external holding tank.

iv) Drainage systems may be manual or automatic. They are to be fitted with antisuction devices.

v) Means are to be provided to limit the volume of gas exhausted with each drainage operation.

iii) Holding Tanks

Holding tanks are to be provided with the following:

i) Means (such as pilot and vent valves) to ensure that the tank pressure does not exceed the PVHO pressure during operation of the flushing/drainage systems or during decompression.

ii) Pressure gauge for indicating the tank pressure.

iii) A means for flushing/cleaning the holding tank is to be considered.

iv) Means to drain the tank. The means may be manual or automatic. Automatic means are to be of the fail-to-close type. Manual means are to require two positive manual actions for operation.

v) Where multiple toilets are connected to a common holding tank, means are to be provided to prevent inadvertent depressurization of the chambers and backflow to the other compartments.

3.9 PVHO Support Components

3.9.1 Transfer Trunks/Tunnels

i) Trunks/tunnels incorporated in or created by the coupling of PVHOs are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 4, 5, and 6.

ii) Trunks/tunnels are to be capable of allowing the safe transfer of occupants between PVHOs and are to have a minimum internal diameter of 610 mm (24 inches).

iii) Trunks/tunnels are to be provided with an external means for monitoring, venting and equalizing internal pressure with the compartment being serviced or the eternal atmosphere.

iv) Trunks/tunnels exceeding 914 mm (36 inches) in length are to be provided with internal hand and/or footholds.

3.9.2 Medical/Service Locks

i) Where Medical/Service locks are provided, they are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 4, 5, and 6.

ii) Medical/Service locks are to be sized for their intended purpose. The dimensions of these locks are to be adequate for enabling essential supplies such as Carbon Dioxide (CO2 ) scrubber canisters, diving masks / hel

d purpose. The dimensions of these locks are to be adequate for enabling essential supplies such as Carbon Dioxide (CO2 ) scrubber canisters, diving masks / helmets, etc., to be safely transferred into/out of the PVHOs. As far as practicable, the dimensions are to also minimize the loss of gas when the lock is being used.

iii) Medical/Service lock doors/covers that unseat with pressure are to be provided with locking mechanisms and safety interlocks. The interlocks are to prevent accidental opening of the doors/covers while under pressure and are to prevent pressurization of the locks if the locking mechanisms are not properly closed.

iv) Medical/Service locks are to be provided with an external means for monitoring, venting and equalizing pressure with the compartment being serviced or the external atmosphere.

3.9.3 Mating Systems and Clamping / Closure Arrangements

i) Where mating systems and clamping arrangements are provided, they are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 4, 5, and 6.

ii) Mating systems between underwater units (e.g., Hyperbaric Evacuation Units) and the hyperbaric facility's PVHOs are to enable their connection and disconnection

Mating systems between underwater units (e.g., Hyperbaric Evacuation Units) and the hyperbaric facility's PVHOs are to enable their connection and disconnection easily and securely under the worst-case operating and environmental conditions.

iii) The mating devices are to be of suitable construction, consideration being given to the anticipated loads that may be imposed on them. As a minimum, the loads resulting from the mating forces, worst-case environmental conditions, as well as internal and external pressure are to be considered.

iv) Clamping/Closure arrangements are to be provided with a safety interlock in order to prevent inadvertent opening of the clamps/closures while under pressure and to prevent pressurization of the transfer trunks/tunnels if the clamps/closures are not properly closed. Mechanical locking mechanisms are required for hydraulically or pneumatically actuated clamping/closure arrangements. Means are to be provided to visually indicate to the operator complete locking or unlocking of the clamp/closure.

v) Where a power actuating system is used for the mating operations, a manual or stored mechanical power means is to be provided as a backup to connect or

ure.

v) Where a power actuating system is used for the mating operations, a manual or stored mechanical power means is to be provided as a backup to connect or disconnect the two units in the event of failure of the normal power actuating system.

3.10 Control Stations

i) Control stations for hyperbaric facilities are to meet the applicable requirements of Section 21, "Dive Control Stations".

ii) When shipping containers are used for housing the control stations of hyperbaric facilities, they are to meet the applicable requirements of the ABS Rules for Certification of Cargo Containers or the ABS Guide for Certification of Offshore Containers, based on intended use. Special consideration will be given to containers that are designed and fabricated to other recognized industry standards that are not less effective than the Rules or Guide.

3.11 Utility Vans

When shipping containers are used for housing the machinery, tools, spares, etc., of the hyperbaric facility, they are to meet the applicable requirements of the ABS Rules for Certification of Cargo Containers or the ABS Guide for Certification of Offshore Containers, based on intended use. Special consideration will be given

ules for Certification of Cargo Containers or the ABS Guide for Certification of Offshore Containers, based on intended use. Special consideration will be given to containers that are designed and fabricated to other recognized industry standards that are not less effective than the Rules or Guide.

3.12 Hyperbaric Evacuation

When hyperbaric facilities are provided with means of hyperbaric evacuation and these means are part of the classed hyperbaric facility, then the means of hyperbaric evacuation are to comply with the applicable requirements of Subsection 14/11, "Hyperbaric Evacuation".

3.13 Diving Bells

When diving bells are part of the classed hyperbaric facility (e.g., for diver training centers), then these bells are to comply with the applicable requirements of Subsection 14/7, "Diving Bells".

3.14 Handling Systems

When handling systems are part of the classed hyperbaric facility, then these handling systems are to comply with the applicable requirements of Section 20, "Handling Systems".

3.15 Testing of Fully Outfitted PVHOs

Fully outfitted PVHOs of the hyperbaric facility are to be subjected to the following tests in the presence of the Surveyor:

3.15.1 PVHO Life

Outfitted PVHOs

Fully outfitted PVHOs of the hyperbaric facility are to be subjected to the following tests in the presence of the Surveyor:

3.15.1 PVHO Life Support Qualification Testing

All PVHOs of the hyperbaric facility are to undergo life support qualification testing in the presence of the Surveyor.

ii) For hyperbaric facilities incorporating duplicate PVHOs (100% identical, i.e., same dimensions, same outfitting and same life support systems), such testing is only required for one of the duplicate PVHOs.

Life support testing is to be conducted after the PVHO is completely outfitted. iii)

The tests may be conducted at surface conditions (1 ata) or by pressurizing the PVHO to a iv) pressure corresponding to shallow depths.

When External Environmental Control Units (ECUs) are provided, they are to be tested v) independent of the Carbon Dioxide (CO2) scrubbers within the PVHO.

During testing, the Oxygen (O2) and Carbon Dioxide (CO2) levels, pressure, relative vi) humidity and temperature are to be monitored and recorded at intervals suitable to verify the life support system design but not exceeding 15 minutes. The monitored parameters are to be within acceptable range

recorded at intervals suitable to verify the life support system design but not exceeding 15 minutes. The monitored parameters are to be within acceptable ranges to the satisfaction of the Surveyor.

The testing is to include the following: vii)

i) A manned test with the maximum number of occupants within the PVHO or a simulated test is to be conducted until equilibrium is achieved by the Carbon Dioxide (CO2) scrubbers, plus an additional one hour. This test is to be conducted for a minimum of three hours. Where simulation testing is carried-out, the simulation details are to be submitted for review. The following is to be demonstrated during the test:

Changing-out of the Carbon Dioxide (CO2) absorbent can be achieved ii) without exceeding a Carbon Dioxide (CO2) concentration of 0.015 ata and that the concentration of Carbon Dioxide (CO2) can be reestablished and maintained at or below 0.005 at a following the change-out.

0.005 at a following the change-out.

The test may be continued beyond a Carbon Dioxide (CO2)

3.15.2 PVHO Functional Testing

Functional tests, wherein satisfactory operation is to be demonstrated for the life support systems, piping systems, electrical systems, communication systems, locks, and other essential systems and equipment of the PVHO. This testing is to be conducted at the maximum allowable working pressure using the normal breathing gas.

4 Interface and Installation Arrangements

4.1 Hyperbaric Facilities Installed on Vessels/Offshore Facilities

When hyperbaric facilities are installed on vessels/offshore facilities, they are to comply with the following requirements:

4.1.1 Installation

4.1.1(a) Hazardous Zones

Hyperbaric facilities are to be installed in safe areas on the vessel / offshore facility. Safe areas are those areas that are outside haza

(a) Hazardous Zones

Hyperbaric facilities are to be installed in safe areas on the vessel / offshore facility. Safe areas are those areas that are outside hazardous zones 0, 1 and 2 as defined by the International Electrotechnical Commission's Publication No.79-10 and Chapter 6 of the IMO MODU code:

Zone 0: A zone in which an explosive gas-air mixture is continuously present or present for long periods.

Zone 1: A zone in which an explosive gas-air mixture is likely to occur in normal operation.

Zone 2: A zone in which an explosive gas-air mixture is not likely to occur, and if it occurs it will only exist for a short time.

Special consideration will be given on a case-by-case basis for diving systems that may be installed in Zone 2 hazardous zones.

4.1.1(b) Angles of Inclination

For hyperbaric facilities installed on vessels or ship-shaped offshore facilities, the PVHOs, machinery and systems essential for the safe operation of the hyperbaric facility are to be designed for operation under the inclinations specified in TABLE 2 below:

4.1.1(d)

Hyperbaric facilities and breathing gas storage facilities are not to be installed in machinery spaces that have machinery not associated with the hyperbaric facility.

4.1.1(e)

Hyperbaric facilities are to be installed in spaces or locations that are adequately ventilated and provided with suitable lighting. Hyperbaric facilities are not to be installed in the vicinity of exhausts / ventilation outlets from machinery spaces and galleys.
