The primary system is to be supplied by two independent sources of power, one of which is to be the emergency source of power. Speech unscramblers are to be provided when mixed gas is used.

5.11.2(c)

The secondary system is to be fully independent of the primary system. It may consist of a soundpowered telephone or other means that are capable of operating in the event of cable/umbilical failure or loss of main and emergency power.

5.13 Fire Fighting and Fire Detection

5.13.1 Internal Fire Extinguishing Systems for PVHOs

5.13.1(a) General

i) Each compartment of a PVHO is to be provided with suitable means for fire fighting. This may include fixed

xtinguishing Systems for PVHOs

5.13.1(a) General

i) Each compartment of a PVHO is to be provided with suitable means for fire fighting. This may include fixed fire extinguishing systems or portable fire extinguishers.

ii) For PVHOs intended for hyperbaric applications, the means of fire fighting, including any portable hyperbaric fire extinguishers, are to be suitable for operation at all internal pressures until the maximum operating pressure of the PVHO.

iii) Chemical extinguishing agents/propellants with toxic or narcotic effects for any operating conditions are not permitted. Salt water is not to be used as an extinguishing agent/ propellant. Choking hazards of extinguishing agents/propellants are to be taken into consideration. Extinguishing agents/propellants that are carcinogenic, mutagenic, or teratogenic at the expected concentrations during use are not permitted.

iv) The capacity of extinguishing systems using gaseous agents/propellants is to be such that complete discharge of the extinguishing system will not result in a toxic concentration.

v) Consideration is to be given to the increase in compartment pressure resulting from the discharge of the extinguishing age

result in a toxic concentration.

v) Consideration is to be given to the increase in compartment pressure resulting from the discharge of the extinguishing agents/propellants.

5.13.1(b) Fixed Fire Extinguishing Systems

i) Fixed fire extinguishing systems are to be capable of providing rapid and efficient distribution of the extinguishing agent to any part of the compartment under all foreseeable conditions.

ii) For hyperbaric applications, the fixed fire extinguishing systems are to be suitable for operation at all internal pressures until the maximum operating pressure of the PVHO.

iii) The systems are to be manually actuated. Means are to be provided to actuate the systems from within and outside the PVHOs. Suitable safeguards are to be provided to prevent inadvertent actuation.

iv) The extinguishing agent is to be fresh water or other liquid agents suitable for use in manned compartments.

v) For hyperbaric applications, the systems are to be capable of recharging without the need for depressurizing the PVHOs.

vi) The systems are to have the capability to discharge less than the total supply of the extinguishing agent.

vii) Fixed extinguishing systems are to be functiona

The systems are to have the capability to discharge less than the total supply of the extinguishing agent.

vii) Fixed extinguishing systems are to be functionally tested in the presence of the Surveyor. For hyperbaric applications, the extinguishing system is to be functionally tested over the full range of internal pressures until the maximum operating pressure of the PVHO.

5.13.1(c) Portable Fire Extinguishers

i) When PVHOs are not provided with fixed fire extinguishing systems, the fire extinguishing means is to consist of portable fire extinguishers.

ii) For hyperbaric applications, the portable fire extinguishers are to be suitable for operation at all internal pressures until the maximum operating pressure of the PVHO.

iii) Portable fire extinguishers, including hyperbaric fire extinguishers, are to be of an approved type. The extinguishers are to be provided with documentation confirming their approval by an appropriate third-party independent agency or laboratory for marine services.

iv) The extinguishing agent is to be distilled water or other non-conductive liquid agents suitable for use in manned compartments.

5.13.2 Internal Fire Detection and Alarm Systems for P

is to be distilled water or other non-conductive liquid agents suitable for use in manned compartments.

5.13.2 Internal Fire Detection and Alarm Systems for PVHOs

i) Each compartment of the PVHO is to be provided with automatic fire detection and alarm systems.

ii) For hyperbaric applications, the fire detection and alarm systems are to be suitable for operation of the PVHO. Where such systems are unsuitable, consideration will be given to alternate arrangements.

iii) Faults (voltage failure, breakage in the detection loops, earth faults, etc.) in the fire detection and alarm systems are to be indicated at the habitat's control station by means of visual and audible signals.

5.15 PVHO Layout and Outfitting

5.15.1 General

i) Each compartment of the PVHO is to be provided with means of visual monitoring of the occupants from the control station. At least one viewport is to be provided for each compartment.

ii) PVHOs are to be provided with adequate means of normal and emergency lighting of sufficient luminosity to allow the occupants to read gauges and operate essential systems within each compartment safely. A general interior illumination level of at least 270 lumens/m2 (2

ow the occupants to read gauges and operate essential systems within each compartment safely. A general interior illumination level of at least 270 lumens/m2 (25 foot-candles) is to be provided, with 540 lumens/m2 (50 foot-candles) provided over bunks and in work areas under normal operating conditions.

iii) PVHOs are to be provided with suitable means of heating, cooling and controlling humidity, to maintain a comfortable temperature and humidity level for the occupants. The heating, cooling and humidity control systems are to have full redundancy.

5.15.2 Habitats used for Hyperbaric Applications

Habitats used for hyperbaric applications are to comply with the following:

i) PVHOs or PVHO compartments that serve as living facilities and other compartments that may be used for decompression are to be provided with suitable medical/equipment locks. See 17/5.19.3 below.

ii) All compartments of PVHOs are to be provided with gas circulation systems. The circulation systems are to have the capability to maintain homogenous gas mixtures and avoid stratification of gas layers within the compartments.

5.15.3 PVHOs Continuously Occupied for 12 Hours or Longer

When PVHOs are continuous

s mixtures and avoid stratification of gas layers within the compartments.

5.15.3 PVHOs Continuously Occupied for 12 Hours or Longer

When PVHOs are continuously occupied for 12 hours or longer, they are to be provided with the following features:

5.15.3(a)

PVHOs that serve as living facilities are to be provided with one bunk for each occupant. The dimensions of the bunks are to be suitable for enabling the occupants to rest comfortably.

5.15.3(b)

The viewports/windows for PVHOs that serve as living facilities are to allow the bunks to be seen as much as possible over their entire lengths from the outside.

5.15.3(c)

PVHOs that serve as living facilities are to have internal dimensions sufficient to allow most occupants to stand upright and to lie down and stretch out comfortably on their bunks.

5.15.3(d)

PVHOs or PVHO compartments that serve as transfer locks are to have internal dimensions sufficient to allow the simultaneous transfer of at least two persons. The floodable volume is not to be less than 105 ft3 (3.0 m3 ).

5.15.3(e)

Toilet, hand washing, and shower arrangements are to be provided and are to be installed outside the living facilities in separate compartme

0 m3 ).

5.15.3(e)

Toilet, hand washing, and shower arrangements are to be provided and are to be installed outside the living facilities in separate compartments.

5.15.3(f)

For hyperbaric applications, the toilet, hand washing, and shower arrangements are to comply with the following:

i) Flush-type toilets, when installed, are to be provided with the following:

a) An interlock to prevent flushing of the toilet while the user is seated.

b) Stand-offs on the toilet seat to prevent the formation of a complete seal between the toilet bowl and the user.

c) Water for flushing at a sufficient pressure above the internal pressure of the PVHO at the time of flushing.

d) Flushing systems are to require two positive manual actions for operation.

e) Flushing systems are to have means to limit the volume of gas exhausted with each flushing operation.

f) Effluent drain from the toilets is to be routed to an external holding tank.

g) Means for communicating with the control station are to be provided in the vicinity of flush type toilets.

ii) Hand washing and shower systems are to be provided with the following:

a) A sink for hand washing and a shower head for showering.

b) Water a

pe toilets.

ii) Hand washing and shower systems are to be provided with the following:

a) A sink for hand washing and a shower head for showering.

b) Water at a sufficient pressure above the internal pressure of the PVHO at the time of washing or showering.

c) Effluent drain from the hand washing and shower systems is to be routed to an external holding tank.

d) Drainage systems may be manual or automatic. They are to be fitted with antisuction devices.

e) Means are to be provided to limit the volume of gas exhausted with each drainage operation.

iii) Holding tanks are to be provided with the following:

a) Means (such as pilot and vent valves) to ensure that the tank pressure does not exceed the PVHO pressure during operation of the flushing/drainage systems or during decompression.

b) Pressure gauge for indicating the tank pressure.

c) Means to determine the tank level.

d) A means for flushing/cleaning the holding tank is to be considered.

e) Means to drain the tank. The means may be manual or automatic. Automatic means are to be of the fail-to-close type. Manual means are to require two positive manual actions for operation.

5.17 Diver Lock-Out Compartments

5.17.1 G

ic means are to be of the fail-to-close type. Manual means are to require two positive manual actions for operation.

5.17 Diver Lock-Out Compartments

5.17.1 General

i) Diver lock-out compartments are to be provided with adequate internal space for the maximum number of occupants (divers) envisaged, together with their equipment. A minimum of two divers are to be considered for all designs.

ii) When it is intended that diver decompression be carried out within the lock-out compartments, the dimensions of lock-out compartments are to be sufficient to permit decompression to be carried out in an uncramped position.

iii) Seating is to be provided for the maximum number of occupants.

iv) Suitable means are to be provided for taking unconscious divers into lock-out compartments.

v) Hand and/or footholds are to be provided in the vicinity of lock-out hatches to assist divers in entering/exiting the lock-out compartments.

5.17.2 Piping Systems

Where the design of lock-out compartments is such that a vacuum could be inadvertently drawn while draining/exhausting the compartments, suitable means of under-pressure protection (such as vacuum breakers) are to be provided.

ently drawn while draining/exhausting the compartments, suitable means of under-pressure protection (such as vacuum breakers) are to be provided.

5.17.3 Life Support and Environmental Control Systems

5.17.3(a)

For the lock-out compartment, the normal and reserve capacity of the life support system as specified under 17/5.7.1.vi above, is to be sufficient to supply the maximum number of occupants (divers) plus one with the appropriate breathing gases and provide CO2 removal.

5.17.3(b)

When it is intended that diver decompression be carried out within the lock-out compartments, a sufficient supply of life support gases required for the decompression period (in accordance with the applicable decompression table) is to be available for the expected maximum number of Occupants (divers) plus one.

5.17.3(c)

Means for overboard dumping or storage of the lock-out compartment gases are to be provided.

5.17.3(d)

Under normal operating conditions, diver decompression is to be controlled from the control station. Additional decompression controls may be provided in the lock-out compartments for use during an emergency.

5.17.3(e)

An appropriate supply of treatment gases (in accordance

mpression controls may be provided in the lock-out compartments for use during an emergency.

5.17.3(e)

An appropriate supply of treatment gases (in accordance with the applicable treatment table) is to be available for the treatment of the occupants (divers).

5.17.3(f)

For the lock-out compartment, in-lieu of the emergency life support system capacity specified under 17/5.7.4.vi above, the capacity of the emergency life support system is to be as follows:

a) Capacity that is sufficient to supply the maximum number of occupants (divers) plus one on BIBS supply for 150 percent of the time normally required to decompress to the surface from the maximum operating depth, but not less than 2 hours. A respiratory minute volume (RMV) of not less than 12 liters/minute or 0.42 actual cubic feet/minute (acfm) per diver for average rest conditions and/or 22.5 liters/minute or 0.8 acfm per diver for light work conditions at ambient pressure is to be used for determining the capacity.

b) Capacity that is sufficient to de-water a lock-out compartment filled with 40% water, while at a pressure corresponding to the maximum operating depth.

ii) In-Water Divers Supplied from Umbilicals.

k-out compartment filled with 40% water, while at a pressure corresponding to the maximum operating depth.

ii) In-Water Divers Supplied from Umbilicals.

a) Capacity that is sufficient to supply the maximum number of occupants (divers) plus one on BIBS supply for 150 percent of the time normally required to decompress to the surface from the maximum operating depth, but not less than 2 hours. A respiratory minute volume (RMV) of not less than 12 liters/minute or 0.42 actual cubic feet/minute (acfm) per diver for average rest conditions and/or 22.5 liters/minute or 0.8 acfm per diver for light work conditions at ambient pressure is to be used for determining the capacity.

b) Capacity that is sufficient to supply each of the divers with breathing gas for at least 15 minutes when they are outside the lock-out compartment. A respiratory minute volume (RMV) of not less than 45 liters/minute or 1.5 actual cubic feet/ minute (acfm) per diver for the lock-out divers under heavy work conditions and 62.5 liters/minute or 2.2 acfm per diver for the stand-by diver under severe work conditions at ambient pressure is to be used for determining the capacity.

r diver for the stand-by diver under severe work conditions at ambient pressure is to be used for determining the capacity.

5.17.3(g) Diver Umbilicals

i) Diver umbilicals, when provided, are to be supplied with breathing gases from both the normal life support system as well as the emergency life support system. Each diver's gas supply is to be arranged so that failure of one umbilical does not interfere with another diver's breathing gas supply.

ii) Suitable means are to be provided for stowing the diver umbilicals on the habitat. The means of stowing is to consider the minimum bend radius of the umbilical.

iii) The primary breathing gas supply to the stand-by diver's umbilical is to be independent of the primary breathing gas supply to the lock-out diver's umbilicals. The primary breathing gas to the stand-by diver may be supplied from the normal or the emergency life support system and is to be sufficient to allow the stand-by diver to exit the lock-out compartment and recover an injured diver. The secondary breathing gas

fe support system and is to be sufficient to allow the stand-by diver to exit the lock-out compartment and recover an injured diver. The secondary breathing gas supply to the stand-by diver may be common with the lock-out diver's primary breathing gas supply, provided it has the capability to continue supplying breathing gas to the stand-by diver if the working diver's umbilical fails.

iv) Breathing gas supplies are to be arranged so that blowing-down or flushing of the lockout compartment does not interfere with the breathing gas supplied to any diver outside the lock-out compartment.

5.17.3(h) Temperature and Humidity Control

i) A heating system, when provided, is to maintain the temperature of the lock-out compartment internal atmosphere and the umbilical supplied in-water divers at a comfortable level. Suitable safety features are to be provided for preventing overheating.

ii) Suitable means are to be provided for isolating the heating arrangement of the lock-out compartment from the heating arrangement for the umbilical supplied in-water divers.

iii) For diving operations exceeding 500 FSW (152 MSW), suitable means are to be provided for heating the inspired gases of umbi

ical supplied in-water divers.

iii) For diving operations exceeding 500 FSW (152 MSW), suitable means are to be provided for heating the inspired gases of umbilical supplied in-water divers. Suitable safety features are to be provided for preventing overheating.

5.19 PVHO Support Components

5.19.1 Transfer Trunks/Tunnels

i) Trunks/tunnels incorporated in or created by the coupling of the habitat's PVHOs are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 4, 5, and 6.

ii) Trunks/tunnels are to be capable of allowing the safe transfer of occupants between the habitat's PVHOs and are to have a minimum internal diameter of 610 mm (24 inches).

iii) Trunks/tunnels are to be provided with an external means for monitoring, venting and equalizing internal pressure with the compartment being serviced or the eternal atmosphere.

iv) Trunks/tunnels exceeding 914 mm (36 inches) in length are to be provided with internal hand and/or footholds.

5.19.2 Medical/Service Locks

i) Where Medical/Service locks are provided, they are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 4, 5, and 6.

ii) Medical/Service locks are to be sized for their intended purpose.

d, they are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 4, 5, and 6.

ii) Medical/Service locks are to be sized for their intended purpose. The dimensions of these locks are to be adequate for enabling essential supplies such as CO2 scrubber canisters, diving masks/helmets, etc., to be safely transferred into/out of the habitat's PVHOs. As far as practicable, the dimensions are to also minimize the loss of gas when the lock is being used.

iii) Medical/Service lock doors/covers that unseat with pressure are to be provided with locking mechanisms and safety interlocks. The interlocks are to prevent accidental opening of the doors/covers while under pressure and are to prevent pressurization of the locks if the locking mechanisms are not properly closed.

iv) Medical/Service locks are to be provided with an external means for monitoring, venting and equalizing pressure with the compartment being serviced or the external atmosphere.

5.19.3 Mating Systems and Clamping/Closure Arrangements

i) Where mating systems and clamping arrangements are provided, they are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 4, 5, and 6.

ii) Mating systems betwee

ystems and clamping arrangements are provided, they are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Sections 4, 5, and 6.

ii) Mating systems between underwater units (such as diving bells, submersibles, etc.) and the habitat's PVHOs are to enable their connection and disconnection easily and securely under the worst expected environmental conditions. (See 20/55.1 TABLE 1).

iii) The mating devices are to be of suitable construction, consideration being given to the anticipated loads that may be imposed on them. As a minimum, the loads resulting from the mating forces, worst case environmental conditions, as well as internal and external pressure are to be considered.

iv) Clamping/closure arrangements are to be provided with a safety interlock in order to prevent inadvertent opening of the clamps/closures while under pressure and to prevent pressurization of the transfer trunks/tunnels if the clamps/closures are not properly closed. Mechanical locking mechanisms are required for hydraulically or pneumatically actuated clamping/closure arrangements. Means are to be provided to visually indicate to the operator complete locking or unlocking of the clamp/closure.

v) Wher

tuated clamping/closure arrangements. Means are to be provided to visually indicate to the operator complete locking or unlocking of the clamp/closure.

v) Where a power actuating system is used for the mating operations, a manual or stored mechanical power means is to be provided as a backup to connect or disconnect the two units in the event of failure of the normal power actuating system.

5.21 Testing of Fully Outfitted PVHOs

Fully outfitted PVHOs of the habitat are to be subjected to the following tests in the presence of the Surveyor:

5.21.1 Life Support System Tests

Life support system tests as per the applicable requirements of 17/5.7.6 above.

5.21.2 Functional Tests

Functional tests, wherein satisfactory operation is to be demonstrated for the life support systems, piping systems, electrical systems, communication systems, locks, etc. For hyperbaric applications, this testing is to be conducted at the maximum allowable working pressure using the normal breathing gas.

7 Underwater Complexes

7.1 General

Underwater complexes are to meet the requirements specified under 17/5 above.

7.3 Submersibles or Lock-Out Submersibles or Ambient-Pressure Submersibles

Where subme

ater complexes are to meet the requirements specified under 17/5 above.

7.3 Submersibles or Lock-Out Submersibles or Ambient-Pressure Submersibles

Where submersibles or lock-out submersibles or ambient-pressure submersibles are part of the underwater complex, they are to meet the requirements of 13, respectively.

7.5 Diving Bells

Where diving bells are part of the underwater complex, they are to meet the requirements of Section 14.

7.7 Handling Systems

Where handling systems are part of the underwater complex, they are to meet the requirements of Section 20.

9 Installation and Securing Arrangements

9.1 Securing Arrangements

Underwater habitats and/or underwater complexes are to be are to be provided with the appropriate equipment to securely fasten them to the sea floor. This equipment is to meet the applicable requirements of Section 20 or a recognized national/international standard.

9.3 Installation

The installation location of the underwater habitat/complex, as well as the suitability, stability and loadbearing capacity of the sea floor in-way of the underwater habitat/complex, are the responsibility of the owner/operator.

S E C T I O N 1 8

Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) (2014)

1 Scope (2024)

This Section, along with 1, as applicable, provides the requirements for the design, fabrication and testing of Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) or Hybrid Remotely Operated Vehicles (HROVs).

3 Definitions

3.1 Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV)

An ROV is a tethered unmanned unit designed for functions such as underwater observation, survey, inspection, construction, intervention or other tasks.

3.3 Hybrid Remotely Operated Vehicle (HROV)

A hybrid unit that incorporates features of both ROVs and AUVs and can be operated in either ROV mode or AUV mode.

3.5 ROV or HROV System (2024)

An ROV or HROV System includes the vehicle, as well as the associated support systems such as the handling system and the control station.

5 ROV and HROV Categorization (2024)

5.1 Class I (Pure Observation) (2024)

Pure observation ROVs or HROVs are primarily employed for underwater video observation. In general, they are small vehicles fitted with video cameras, lights and thrusters.

In order to undertake ta

marily employed for underwater video observation. In general, they are small vehicles fitted with video cameras, lights and thrusters.

In order to undertake tasks other than video monitoring, they would need considerable modification.

5.3 Class II (Observation with Payload Option) (2024)

Class II ROVs or HROVs include vehicles capable of carrying additional sensors (over and above those carried by Class I ROVs or HROVs) such as data measurement instrumentation, sonar systems, additional video cameras, still cameras, etc. In general, they are somewhat larger than Class I vehicles.

Class II ROVs or HROVs are capable of carrying additional payload without loss of functional capabilities.

5.5 Class III (Work Class Vehicles) (2024)

Work class ROVs or HROVs are primarily employed for conducting work while submerged in the water column. In general, these vehicles are larger and more powerful than Class I and II vehicles. These vehicles are large enough to carry additional sensors and/or manipulators.

Class III vehicles commonly have multiplexing capability that allows additional sensors and tools to be added to the ROV or HROV and operated without the need for being separately hard

nly have multiplexing capability that allows additional sensors and tools to be added to the ROV or HROV and operated without the need for being separately hardwired through the umbilical.

5.7 Class IV (Seabed-working Vehicles) (2024)

Seabed-working vehicles including seabed plows generally operate on the seafloor. These vehicles travel/ maneuver using wheels, belt traction systems, thrusters, water jets or by combinations of these methods.

In general, Class IV vehicles are larger and heavier than Class III vehicles. These vehicles are configured for special purpose tasks. Such tasks typically include cable and pipeline trenching, excavation, dredging or other seafloor construction work.

5.9 Class V (Prototype or Development Vehicles) (2024)

Vehicles in this category include those that are being developed for special applications and those that are regarded as prototypes. Special-purpose vehicles that do not fit into one of the other categories specified above may also be designated as Class V vehicles.

11 Pressure Boundary Components

Pressure boundary components are to be certified by the manufacturer as being suitable for the intended service.

13 External Structures

13.1 General

External structures include all non-pressure retaining structural members located outside metallic pressure boundary components (e.g., frames, hydrodynamic fairings, brackets, lugs, etc.).

In order to avoid the detrimental effects of buoyancy, all free flooding external structures are to be designed so that all their inner spaces

s, lugs, etc.).

In order to avoid the detrimental effects of buoyancy, all free flooding external structures are to be designed so that all their inner spaces are fully floodable and ventable. Suitable openings in the uppermost and lowermost parts of the structures are to be provided. Flood and vent openings are to be properly dimensioned to ensure free circulation of water.

13.3 Corrosion Protection

Metallic external structures are to be provided with appropriate means to manage/mitigate corrosion, marine growth and galvanic action.

13.5 Mechanical Protection

ROVs are to be provided with adequate mechanical protection to protect the critical components during handling operations and other normal or emergency operations.

13.7 Frames

Frames, when provided, are to be of adequate construction, consideration being given to their size and the loads which may be imposed upon them.

Loads to be considered include those which result from bottoming, striking objects, wave slap, bumping alongside the tender, and other loads resulting from being launched and recovered in sea state 6 (see 20/55.1 TABLE 1). A lesser sea state may be considered when it is intended that the ROV be operated

ulting from being launched and recovered in sea state 6 (see 20/55.1 TABLE 1). A lesser sea state may be considered when it is intended that the ROV be operated with a launch and recovery system whose design parameters are less than sea state 6.

When it is intended to carry out hydrostatic testing of pressure boundary components when they are installed on the support frames, the maximum static load due to the hydrostatic testing is to also be considered in the design.

The calculated stresses are not to exceed the allowable stress fa as obtained from the following equation:

13.9 Lifting Lugs/Attachments (2024)

The lifting lugs/attachments used for launch and recovery of ROVs or HROVs are to be designed for forces of at least 1.75g vertical (1g static plus 0.75g dynamic), 0.75g transverse and 0.75g longitudinal, unless otherwise determined, acting simultaneously under the most severe loading condition. Where approp

.75g dynamic), 0.75g transverse and 0.75g longitudinal, unless otherwise determined, acting simultaneously under the most severe loading condition. Where appropriate, the increased loading due to other applicable loads such as entrained water and mud, added mass and drag are to be also considered. Calculated stresses are not to exceed those specified in 18/13.7 above.

Removable equipment on ROVs or HROVs are to be provided with lifting lugs/attachments to facilitate their easy retrieval.

13.11 Towing Attachments (2024)

For towed ROVs or HROVs, an accessible towing point, appropriately sized for the anticipated conditions is to be provided.

15 Nameplates (2024)

ROVs or HROVs are to be fitted with permanent nameplates indicating their design depth, maximum allowable working pressures/temperatures, hydrostatic test pressure, manufacturer's name, serial number, and year built. The nameplates are to be stainless steel or other suitable material and are to be permanently attached to the vehicle.

17 Emergency Recovery Features (2024)

ROVs or HROVs are to be provided with permanent features for the attachment of recovery equipment. It is to be demonstrated by appropriate analysis th

s (2024)

ROVs or HROVs are to be provided with permanent features for the attachment of recovery equipment. It is to be demonstrated by appropriate analysis that recovery feature attachments are adequate for lifting and raising the vehicle to the surface, under the heaviest emergency condition following a casualty. The analysis is to include consideration of entrained water, mud and sand.

19 Variable Ballast Systems

Variable ballast systems, when provided, are to have ballasting/deballasting capabilities and the capability to compensate for all loading conditions.

Hard ballast tanks subject to internal and/or external pressure, when provided, are to meet the requirements of Subsection 18/11 above.

21 Stability (2024)

ROVs or HROVs are to have adequate stability while operating underwater or on the surface.

23 Propulsion and Steering Systems

23.1 General (2024)

ROVs or HROVs are to be provided with appropriate means of propulsion and steering.

23.3 Thrusters

Thrusters are to be certified by the manufacturer as being suitable for the intended service.

25 Navigational Equipment/Systems (2024)

As a minimum, ROVs or HROVs are to be provided with the following navigational e

g suitable for the intended service.

25 Navigational Equipment/Systems (2024)

As a minimum, ROVs or HROVs are to be provided with the following navigational equipment / systems:

i) Cameras for navigation

ii) External lights

iii) Means to determine the depth from the surface

iv) Emergency locating devices (See Subsection 17/25 below)

27 Emergency Locating Devices (2024)

ROVs or HROVs are to be provided with a surface locating device (such as a strobe light) and a subsurface locating device (such as an acoustic pinger, sonar reflector or buoy).

Acoustic subsurface locating devices are to be compatible with the navigational/positing keeping systems onboard the support vessel/offshore platform.

29 Communication Systems (2024)

ROVs or HROVs are to be provided with appropriate means of communication to enable the transmittal of data from the onboard sensors/equipment to and/or from the Control Box/Dive Control Station.

31 Piping Systems

Piping systems, when provided, are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and 9.

Piping systems are to be mechanically protected from damage.

33 Umbilicals/Tethers

33.1 General (2024)

Umbilicals are to be securely attached to

tion and 9.

Piping systems are to be mechanically protected from damage.

33 Umbilicals/Tethers

33.1 General (2024)

Umbilicals are to be securely attached to ROVs or HROVs by means of strength members or strain relief fittings so that individual electrical connections are not subjected to tensile loads.

The length of umbilicals is to be appropriate for the design depth of the vehicle and is to include extra length for re-termination of the umbilicals.

When the strength member of the umbilical supports the weight of the vehicle, the strength member is to meet the applicable requirements of Section 20, "Handling Systems".

33.3 Tether Management System (TMS) (2024)

Tether management systems, when provided, are to meet the following requirements:

i) The TMS is to be provided with an appropriate means to house and secure the vehicle during launch and recovery operations and travel through the water column.

ii) Where applicable, the TMS frame is to be capable of accommodating additional packages or equipment (such as tool packages).

iii) The TMS frame is to meet the applicable requirements of 18/13. Lifting lugs, when installed, are to meet the requirements of 18/13.9. The TMS

packages).

iii) The TMS frame is to meet the applicable requirements of 18/13. Lifting lugs, when installed, are to meet the requirements of 18/13.9. The TMS frame and lifting lugs are to be designed for lifting the fully outfitted vehicle and additional packages when subjected to a dynamic factor of at least

iv) The TMS is to be provided with a tether spooling mechanism to store and deploy the vehicle excursion tether. The spooling mechanism may be fitted on the TMS frame. Means are to be provided for monitoring the length of the excursion tether spooled out by the spooling mechanism.

v) The spooling mechanism is to be designed for the applicable design loads.

35 Electrical Systems (2024)

Electrical systems are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Section 10.

Electrical systems are to be mechanically protected from damage.

When electrical systems use voltages above 1000 V AC or DC, additional precautions are to be taken to protect personnel (see 10/7.3).

When ROVs or HROVs are intended for operating in hazardous areas, the electrical equipment on the vehicle is to meet the requirements of 10/3.5.

37 Access to Equipment

Adequate access space for mainte

ing in hazardous areas, the electrical equipment on the vehicle is to meet the requirements of 10/3.5.

37 Access to Equipment

Adequate access space for maintenance and handling is to be provided around the equipment and tools of ROVs.

39 ROV and HROV Control Boxes or Stations (2024)

Each vehicle or vehicle system is to be provided with a control box or control station for monitoring and controlling the vehicle operations, as applicable.

39.1 ROV or HROV Control Boxes (2024)

ROV or HROV control boxes are to be provided with sufficient controls and displays to efficiently and reliably control the operations and functioning of the vehicle.

39.3 ROV or HROV Control Stations (2024)

i) ROV or HROV control stations, when provided, are to meet the applicable requirements of Section 21, "Dive Control Stations".

ii) Sufficient controls and displays are to be provided in the control stations to efficiently and reliably control the operations and functioning of the vehicle.

iii) Shipping containers used for housing portable ROV or HROV control stations are to meet the applicable requirements of the ABS Rules for Certification of Cargo Containers. Special consideration will be given t

V or HROV control stations are to meet the applicable requirements of the ABS Rules for Certification of Cargo Containers. Special consideration will be given to containers that are designed and fabricated to other recognized industry standards that are not less effective than the Rules.

41 Handling Systems (2024)

Each ROV or HROV System is to be provided with a handling system to ensure safe transportation of the vehicle to and from the subsea work location.

Handling systems for ROV or HROV Systems are to meet the applicable requirements of Section 20, "Handling Systems".

Handling systems for ROV or HROV Systems are to be designed to permit safe operation in the design sea state when the support vessel/offshore facility is rolling, pitching or heaving. The most probable extreme values of amplitude due to predetermined motions of the dive support vessel/offshore facility is to be used in the design.

HROVs are to also meet the appropriate requirements for AUVs (see Section 19), as applicable to the specific design.

Vehicles or vehicle Systems are to be subjected to the following tests in the presence of the Surveyor:

ests (2024)

Vehicles or vehicle Systems are to be subjected to the following tests in the presence of the Surveyor:

ii) Insulation resistance testing of electrical equipment rated at or above 440 V AC or DC

iii) Functional testing of all instrumentation, equipment and systems

iv) Load testing of handling systems

Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUV) (2014)

1 Scope (2024)

This Section, along with 1, as applicable, provides the requirements for the design, fabrication and testing of Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) and Hybrid Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (HAUVs).

3 Definitions

3.1 Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV)

An AUV is an unmanned untethered unit, designed to operate autonomously and carry out functions such as underwater observation, survey, inspection, intervention or other tasks.

3.3 Hybrid Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (HAUV)

A hybrid unit that incorporates features of both AUVs and ROVs and can be operated in either AUV mode or ROV mode.

oth AUVs and ROVs and can be operated in either AUV mode or ROV mode.

External structures include all non-pressure retaining structural members located outside metallic pressure boundary components (e.g., frames, hydrodynamic fairings, brackets, lugs, etc.).

Frames, when provided, are to be of adequate construction, consideration being given to their size and the loads which may be imposed upon them.

A lesser sea state may be considered when it is intended that the AUV be operated with a launch and recovery system whose design parameters are less than sea state 6.

When it is intended to carry out hydrostatic testing of pressure boundary components when they are installed on the support frames, the maximum static load due to the hydrostatic testing is to also be considered in the design.

13.9 Lifting Lugs/Attachments (2024)

The lifting lugs/attachments used for launch and recovery of AUVs or HAUVs, when provided, are to be designed for forces of at least 1.75g vertical (1g static plus 0.75g dynamic), 0.75g transverse and 0.75g longitudinal, unless otherwise determined, acting simultaneously under the most severe loading condition. Where appropriate, the increased loading due to other applicable loads such as entrained water and mud, added mass and drag are to be also considered. Calculated stresses are not to exceed those specified in 19/9.7 above.

15 Nameplates (2024)

AUVs or HAUVs are to be fitted with permanent nameplates indicating their design depth, maximum allowable working pressures/temperatures, hydrosta

lates (2024)

AUVs or HAUVs are to be fitted with permanent nameplates indicating their design depth, maximum allowable working pressures/temperatures, hydrostatic test pressure, manufacturer's name, serial number, and year built. The nameplates are to be stainless steel or other suitable material and are to be permanently attached to the vehicle.

17 Variable Ballast Systems

Variable ballast systems, when provided, are to have ballasting/deballasting capabilities and the capability to compensate for all loading conditions.

Hard ballast tanks subject to internal and/or external pressure, when provided, are to meet the requirements of 19/7 above.

19 Stability (2024)

AUVs or HAUVs are to have adequate stability while operating underwater or on the surface.

The stability analysis for AUVs or HAUVs is to take into consideration the maximum payload that the vehicle is capable of carrying.

21 Emergency Surfacing System (2024)

AUVs or HAUVs are to be provided with an emergency surfacing system or are to be inherently positively buoyant.

The emergency surfacing system, when provided, is to be independent of the normal means of surfacing and is to be capable of operating in the even

ely buoyant.

The emergency surfacing system, when provided, is to be independent of the normal means of surfacing and is to be capable of operating in the event of failure of the main source of power.

23 Propulsion and Steering Systems

23.1 General (2024)

AUVs or HAUVs are to be provided with appropriate means of propulsion and steering.

23.3 Thrusters

Thrusters are to be certified by the manufacturer as being suitable for the intended service.

25 Control Systems

25.1 General (2024)

AUVs or HAUVs are to be provided with the appropriate equipment to facilitate autonomous operation of the vehicle under the design conditions.

25.3 Emergency Operations (2024)

AUV or HAUV control systems are to have the capability to abort the mission and return to the surface/ pre-determined location based on specific commands from the operator or resume the mission when the failure/malfunction has been resolved.

27 Navigational Equipment/Systems (2024)

AUVs or HAUVs are to be provided with the appropriate equipment/systems that enable the vehicle to determine its three dimensional position in hydrospace while on the surface or underwater.

As a minimum, the following navigational equipmen

ble the vehicle to determine its three dimensional position in hydrospace while on the surface or underwater.

As a minimum, the following navigational equipment/systems are to be provided:

i) Means to determine heading and position

ii) Means to determine roll and pitch

iii) Emergency locating devices (See 19/29 below)

29 Emergency Locating Devices (2024)

AUVs or HAUVs are to be provided with a surface locating device (such as a strobe light) and a subsurface locating device (such as an acoustic pinger, sonar reflector or buoy).

31 Piping Systems

Piping systems, when provided, are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Section 9.

Piping systems are to be mechanically protected from damage.

33 Electrical Systems

33.1 General (2024)

Electrical systems are to meet the applicable requirements of this Section and Section 10.

Electrical systems are to be mechanically protected from damage.

When AUVs or HAUVs are intended for operating in hazardous areas, the electrical equipment on the vehicle is to meet the requirements of 10/3.5.

33.3 Power Supply

The on-board source of power is to have adequate capacity for the mission duration.

35 Hybrid Autonomous Un

eet the requirements of 10/3.5.

The on-board source of power is to have adequate capacity for the mission duration.

HAUVs are to also meet the appropriate requirements for ROVs (see Section 19), as applicable to the specific design.

AUVs or HAUVs are to be subjected to the following tests in the presence of the Surveyor:

Handling Systems (2015)

1 Definitions (2009)

1.1 Handling System (Launch and Recovery System) (2002)

A system supporting launch, recovery and other handling operations of underwater units, hyperbaric facilities and their ancillary equipment and may include cranes, booms, masts, frames, davits, foundations, winches and associated hydraulic and electrical systems as necessary for the intended operations.

1.3 Rated Load or Safe Working Load (2009)

The rated load or safe working load is the maximum load that the asse

ems as necessary for the intended operations.

1.3 Rated Load or Safe Working Load (2009)

The rated load or safe working load is the maximum load that the assembled handling system is certified to lift at its rated speed when the outermost layer of rope or umbilical is being wound on the winch drum, under the parameters specified in the equipment specifications (e.g., hydraulic pressures, electrical current, electrical voltages, etc.).

1.5 Design Load (2009)

The design load is the maximum expected load on the handling system which consists of an appropriate combination of the rated load (see 20/1.3), dynamic effects associated with the rated load, weight of the rigging (hooks, blocks, deployed rope, etc.) and other applicable loads such as, wind load, drag, added mass effect and weight of entrained mud and water. See also 20/7.

1.7 Rigging (2009)

Rigging is a general term for all ropes and other gear (hooks, blocks, etc.) used in handling systems.

1.7.1 Running Rope

Running rope consists of moving or movable rope (wire rope, fiber or synthetic line) that passes over sheaves or through rollers and is used for hoisting, lowering or moving equipment.

1.7.2 Standing Rope

Stand

e rope, fiber or synthetic line) that passes over sheaves or through rollers and is used for hoisting, lowering or moving equipment.

1.7.2 Standing Rope

Standing rope consists of non-moving or non-movable rope that provides support to the structures of the handling system such as the A-frame, masts, etc.

1.7.3 Rotation-Resistant Rope

A rope designed to resist spin or rotation under load.

1.9 Latching Mechanism (2015)

A device used to mechanically connect and support the live load (underwater unit) on the handling system, permitting the ropes to be unloaded.

7 Design (2015)

Design calculations are to be based on recognized standards or recognized engineering methods, which are to be clearly referenced in the required calculations. Some recognized analytic methods are contained in "Specifications for the Design, Fabrication, and Erection of

clearly referenced in the required calculations. Some recognized analytic methods are contained in "Specifications for the Design, Fabrication, and Erection of Structural Steel for Buildings Part I," published by the American Institute of Steel Construction and "Specifications for Aluminum Structures," published by the Aluminum Association.

7.1 Factors of Safety

7.1.1 Wire Rope (2009)

The factor of safety is to be not less than 5 for both conventional and rotation-resistant running rope. The factor of safety for standing rope is to be not less than 4.0. These factors of safety are to be based on the design load of the system as compared to the minimum breaking strength of the rope.

7.1.2 Fiber and Synthetic Rope (2009)

Safety factors for fiber and synthetic rope except nylon are to be not less than 7.0 for running rope and 5.0 for standing rope based on the design load of the system as compared to the minimum breaking strength of the rope. Safety factors for nylon rope are to be not less than 9.0 for running rope and 7.0 for standing rope.

7.1.3 Structural Members in Axial Tension or Compression

Individual stress components for members in tension or compression are not to ex

0 for standing rope.

7.1.3 Structural Members in Axial Tension or Compression

Individual stress components for members in tension or compression are not to exceed the allowable stress obtained from the following equations:

$$F_a = F_v / 1.33$$

$$F_a = F_y / 1.33$$ if $F_y / F_u \le 0.7$

$$F_a = (F_y + F_u)/3.25$$ if $F_y/F_u > 0.7$

if $$F_{v}/F_{u} > 0.7$$

where

Fy = minimum specified yield strength of the material

Fu = minimum specified tensile strength of the material

7.1.4 Structural Members in Bending

Individual bending stress for members in bending is not to exceed the allowable stress specified in 20/7.1.3.

7.1.5 Structural Members Subject to Shear

The shear stress for members subject to shear is not to exceed the allowable stress obtained from the following equation:

$$F_s = 0.577F_a$$

7.1.6 Structural Members Subject to Combined Axial Compression and Bending

When structural members are subjected to axial compression in combination with compression due to bending the computed stresses are to comply with the following requirement:

$$(f_a/F_a') + (f_b/F_b) \le 1.0$$

where

$f_a$ = computed axial compressive stress

$f_h$ = computed bending stress (compressive

h the following requirement:

$$(f_a/F_a') + (f_b/F_b) \le 1.0$$

where

$f_a$ = computed axial compressive stress

$f_h$ = computed bending stress (compressive)

$F'_{\alpha}$ = allowable compressive stress, which is to be the least of the following:

i) $F_a$ value as obtained from 20/7.1.3 for axial stress.

ii) F value as obtained from 20/7.1.11 for buckling.

$F_b$ = allowable compressive stress due to bending as specified in 20/7.1.4.

Note:

Above criterion for combined axial compression and compression due to bending is applicable when $f_a/F_a'$ is less than or equal to 0.15. Otherwise, formulation in Section 1.6 of AISC "Specifications for the Design Fabrication and Erection of Structural Steel for Buildings Part 1" is to be followed.

7.1.7 Structural Members Subject to Axial Tension and Bending

When structural members are subjected to axial tension combined with tension due to bending, the computed stresses are to comply with the following requirement:

$$f_a + f_b \le F_a$$

where $F_a$ is defined in 20/7.1.3.

7.1.8 Riveted Joints

Rivets are not to be subjected to tension. Riveted joints are to have at least two rivets aligned in the direction of the force. The comp

7.1.8 Riveted Joints

Rivets are not to be subjected to tension. Riveted joints are to have at least two rivets aligned in the direction of the force. The computed shear stress is not to exceed the allowable stress, $F_s$ , as obtained from the following equations:

$$F_s = 0.6F_a$$ (single shear)

$$F_s = 0.8F_a$$ (multiple shear)

The computed bearing pressure on walls of holes is not to exceed the allowable stress, $F_N$ , as obtained from the following equations:

$$F_N = 1.5F_a$$ (single shear)

$$F_N = 2.0F_a$$ (multiple shear)

7.1.9 Bolted Joints

The computed stresses in bolts are not to exceed the following allowable values:

Tension: $F_T = 0.65F_a$

Shear: $F_s = 0.6F_a$ (single shear)

$F_s = 0.8F_a$ (multiple shear)

Combined: $(F_T^2 + 3F_s^2)^{1/2} = F_a$

The computed bearing pressure on walls of holes is not to exceed the allowable stress, $F_N$ , as obtained from 20/7.1.8

7.1.10 Structural Members Subject to Crippling

The computed crippling of the structural member is not to exceed 75% of the yield stress.

7.1.11 Structural Members Subject to Buckling

When buckling of a structural member due to compressive or shear stresses or both is a consideration, the co

ss.

7.1.11 Structural Members Subject to Buckling

When buckling of a structural member due to compressive or shear stresses or both is a consideration, the compressive or shear stress is not to exceed the allowable stress, F, as obtained from the following equations:

$F = F_{cr}/1.25$ (for flat members)

$F = F_{cr}/1.55$ (for curved members)

where

$F_{cr}$ = critical buckling stress in compression or shear of the structural member, appropriate to its dimensional configuration, boundary condition, loading pattern, material, etc.

7.1.12 Aluminum

Tensile and yield strengths for welded aluminum alloys are to be in accordance with 5/9.3.

7 1 13 Chains

Chains are to have a safety factor of 4.5 based on their minimum specified ultimate strength. Chains are not to be subjected to torsional loads.

7.3 Design Loads (2008)

The following loads and forces are to be taken into account when designing structural members and joints.

7.3.1 Dead Load

The minimum dead load assumed in design is to consist of the weight of structural parts of the launch and recovery system and materials permanently attached to the structure.

7.3.2 Live Load

The live load is to be based on the maximum wei

l parts of the launch and recovery system and materials permanently attached to the structure.

7.3.2 Live Load

The live load is to be based on the maximum weight in air of the underwater vessel or related systems together with all weights, including personnel, tools, consumables, and water in the vessel to be carried by the system.

7.3.3 Other Loads

Added mass, entrained water and mud, etc.

7.3.4 Dynamic Loads

These are loads produced by accelerations in the vertical, longitudinal and transverse directions. As a minimum, loads resulting from simultaneous accelerations of 1 g (in addition to static gravitational acceleration – a total of 2 g) vertical, 1 g transverse, and 1 g longitudinal are to be used for design except for handling systems intended solely for units not associated with manned operations (e.g., ROV launch and recovery systems) in which case the foregoing minimum dynamic loads may be reduced by 25 percent (to 1.75 g, 0.75 g and 0.75 g). For permanently installed systems, consideration may be given to lesser loads where it can be shown that the maximum expected loads are less than those given above.

7.3.5 Wind Forces

The wind load on the projected area of the

ser loads where it can be shown that the maximum expected loads are less than those given above.

7.3.5 Wind Forces

The wind load on the projected area of the structure is to be considered as a design assumption at a value appropriate to the design conditions.

7.3.6 Maximum Forces

Structural members are to be determined using the maximum appropriate combination of the loads and factors of safety above.

7.5 Tolerances (2015)

For latching mechanism components that are subject to wear while in-service, the designer is to specify the allowable in-service tolerances on the drawings and in the handling system maintenance manual.

9 Power Systems

Power systems and equipment are to be designed for 100% of the design load. Electric motors may have continuous ratings less than ratings corresponding to the design load and suitable short time ratings not less than the design load when such ratings are supported by the design analysis.

11 Power Sources (2015)

For personnel handling, an independent emergency power source is to be available for supplying power in case of failure of the primary source of power. (See 10/5).

13 Sheave and Drum Sizes

The minimum ratio of pitch diameter to r

available for supplying power in case of failure of the primary source of power. (See 10/5).

Wire rope Application Ratio, min. Load block sheaves 16:1 Load hoisting sheaves 18:1 Load hoisting drums 18:1 Boom hoisting sheaves 15:1 Boom hoisting drums 15:1 Fiber and synthetic rope All applications 8:1

15 Rope

Rope is to be constructed in accordance with a recognized standard applicable to the intended service such as API Specification 9A and Federal Specification RR-W-410a.

17 Hooks (2015)

Loose gear hooks for launch and recovery of manned underwater units, with the exception of manned submersibles, are to be equipped with a latch that is positively lockable.

19 Winches (2011)

19.1 Design

Winches are to meet the applicable design requirements of 20/7.

19.3 Operation

Winches are to satisfactorily operate when handling the rated load at the rated speed.

19.5 Power Drives

Lowering of loads is to be controlled by power drives independent of brake mechanisms.

19.7 Drum Capacity

Winch drums are to be capable of accepting the full length of rope being

to be controlled by power drives independent of brake mechanisms.

19.7 Drum Capacity

Winch drums are to be capable of accepting the full length of rope being used. Not less than 5 full wraps of rope is to remain on the drum under any operating condition. The drum flange is to extend a minimum distance of 2.5 times the diameter of the rope over the outermost layer, unless additional means of keeping the rope on the drum are provided (keeper plates, rope guards, etc.).

19.9 Spooling

For recovery systems, appropriate means are to be provided to ensure that the rope being recovered is correctly spooled on the winch drum.

19.11 Emergency Lifting Devices (2014)

Recovery systems for subsurface recovery of tethered manned underwater units are to be provided with a secondary means (such as a guide wire winch and guide wire) for lifting the appropriate design load to the surface, except where alternative lifting equipment adequate for the design load is provided with the underwater system.

19.13 Braking Mechanisms (2014)

Winches for personnel lifting are to be provided with two independent braking mechanisms.

All braking mechanisms are to set automatically upon loss of power to the

hes for personnel lifting are to be provided with two independent braking mechanisms.

All braking mechanisms are to set automatically upon loss of power to the winch.

All braking mechanisms are to be designed to hold 100% of the design load with the outermost layer of rope on the drum.

Hydraulic counterbalance valves, when used as dynamic braking mechanisms, are to meet the following additional requirements:

i) The valves are to be designed and built to a recognized industry standard.

ii) The valves are to be integrally connected to the hydraulic motor (No hoses are permitted between the counterbalance valve and the motor).

iii) The valves are to require positive pressure to release and are to actuate automatically in the event of loss of power.

iv) The valves are to be effective throughout the operating temperature range of the hydraulic fluid.

19.15 Testing (2014)

Testing of the winches is to be conducted in the presence of the Surveyor and is to demonstrate that rated line pull can be achieved at rated speed with the outermost layer of rope on the drum.

All braking mechanisms are to be independently load tested.

Static braking mechanisms are to be statically load test

the outermost layer of rope on the drum.

All braking mechanisms are to be independently load tested.

Static braking mechanisms are to be statically load tested to 100% of the vertical design load.

The load testing of dynamic braking mechanisms is to include functional (dynamic) testing to 125% of the rated load as well as static load testing to 100% of the vertical design load.

21 Latching Mechanisms (2015)

i) When latching mechanisms for personnel handling are installed in the primary load path, they are to be provided with a means to visually indicate complete latching to the operator.

ii) Latching mechanisms for personnel handling are to be statically and functionally load tested as per 20/43.3 and 20/45, respectively.

23 Hydraulic Cylinders (2015)

23.1 Primary Load Path Cylinders

i) Hydraulic cylinders in the primary load path, regardless of cylinder diameter, are to be designed, fabricated and tested in accordance with Section 4-4-1 of the Marine Vessel Rules. Alternatively, the cylinders are to comply with a recognized standard or code for fluid power cylinders or pressure vessels, having factors of safety not less than those specified in the Marine Vessel Rules.

i

recognized standard or code for fluid power cylinders or pressure vessels, having factors of safety not less than those specified in the Marine Vessel Rules.

ii) In addition to 20/23.1.i above, the hydraulic cylinders are to also meet the applicable structural factors of safety specified in 20/7.1.

iii) Hydraulic cylinders are to be designed for the forces induced by the loads specified in 20/7.3.

iv) Ordinary cast iron having an elongation of less than 12% is not to be used for primary load path cylinders.

v) Certified mill test reports for the hydraulic cylinder materials are to be made available to the Surveyor upon request.

vi) Each individual hydraulic cylinder is to be hydrostatically pressure tested to 1.5 times the maximum allowable working pressure (2 times for cast iron and nodular iron cylinders) by the manufacturer.

vii) Counterbalance Valves: Hydraulic cylinders for personnel handling are to be provided with integrally mounted counterbalance valves (lock valves) that are capable of maintaining the position of the cylinder ram in the event of loss of hydraulic power. These valves are to be directly mounted on the cylinders without the use of hoses. The valves are

of the cylinder ram in the event of loss of hydraulic power. These valves are to be directly mounted on the cylinders without the use of hoses. The valves are to be capable of closing automatically when the control lever is returned to the neutral position or upon loss of hydraulic power. These valves are to be rated for the maximum pressure induced by the loads specified in 20/7.3.

23.3 Non-Load Path Cylinders

Non-load path hydraulic cylinders are to comply with a recognized standard or code for fluid power cylinders or pressure vessels. Acceptance of these cylinders will be based on manufacturer's certification of compliance and on verification of permanent identification on each cylinder bearing the manufacturer's name or trademark, standard of compliance and the maximum allowable working pressure and temperature.

25 Hydraulic Accumulators (2015)

Hydraulic accumulators having operating pressures above 6.9 bar (7 kgf/cm2 or 100 psi) are to be certified by ABS in accordance with Section 4-4-1 of the Marine Vessel Rules as pressure vessels, regardless of their diameter.

Each accumulator which may be isolated from the hydraulic system is to be protected by its own relief valve

as pressure vessels, regardless of their diameter.

Each accumulator which may be isolated from the hydraulic system is to be protected by its own relief valve or equivalent. Where a gas charging system is used, a relief valve is to be also provided on the gas side of the accumulator.

27 In-Air Personnel Rescue (2015)

Handling systems for personnel handling are to be provided with emergency means for the recovery of the live load (underwater unit) from any position while in-air. In the event of power failure or control systems failure, the emergency means is to facilitate controlled slewing, luffing or lowering (as applicable), to land the live load safely.

29 Motion Compensation Systems (2017)

29.1 General Requirements

29.1.1 Design

i) The manufacturer is to specify the design/operational parameters of the motion compensation system, including any specific operational limitations.

ii) Motion compensation systems are to be designed so that a single failure in the system does not cause loss of control of the load. Compliance with this requirement is to be verified by means of a risk analysis (See 20/29.1.2 below) or equivalent means.

iii) When motion compensation systems su

pliance with this requirement is to be verified by means of a risk analysis (See 20/29.1.2 below) or equivalent means.

iii) When motion compensation systems subject components (such as the sheaves) and ropes to higher fatigue cycles over the lifetime of the handling system, then the manufacturer is to demonstrate suitability of these components and ropes for the anticipated fatigue cycles. When wire ropes are used, the sheaves and winch drums of motion compensation systems are to have a pitch diameter of not less than 20 times the nominal diameter of the wire rope.

iv) The effect of adding a motion compensation system to the handling system is to be taken into account in the design of the handling system. This could include additional loading on the structural and/or mechanical components of the handling system.

29.1.2 Risk Analysis

For motion compensation systems, a risk analysis is to be carried out for evaluating and mitigating the potential risks associated with the malfunctioning or failure of compensation system components. The risk analysis is to be conducted as per the ABS Guidance Notes on Risk Assessment Applications for the Marine and Offshore Industries or equivalen

mponents. The risk analysis is to be conducted as per the ABS Guidance Notes on Risk Assessment Applications for the Marine and Offshore Industries or equivalent recognized national or international standards.

29.1.3 Structural Members, Machinery, Mechanical Components and Systems

i) Passive heave compensation systems are to be designed to operate using stored energy.

ii) When the passive heave compensation system employs hydraulic/pneumatic stored energy, then the pressure retaining components of the system (such as the accumulators, cylinders and piping systems) are to be designed for the maximum pressure corresponding to the worst case anticipated loading on the system, including dynamic loading where applicable.

iii) Passive heave compensation system components that are located in the primary load path (such as in-line hydraulic cylinders) are to be also designed to meet the applicable structural factors of safety of 20/7.1, when subjected to the worst case anticipated loading, including dynamic loading.

iv) Passive heave compensation systems utilizing hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders and accumulators are to have a position indicator in order to provide the operator with visu

heave compensation systems utilizing hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders and accumulators are to have a position indicator in order to provide the operator with visual indication of the position of the system with reference to its operating range.

29.5 Active Heave Compensation Systems

i) Active heave compensation systems are to be provided with two independent control systems and power supplies. Alternatively, they are to be provided with a back-up means (such as a passive heave compensation system or shock absorber system) to prevent shock loading or structural overloading of the handling system in the event of failure of the active heave compensation system.

ii) Active heave compensation systems are to operate when the handling system is in the offboard condition. Appropriate means are to be provided to prevent operation of the heave compensation system when the handling system is in the onboard condition.

iv) Audio-visual alarms are to be provided for warning the operator in the event of equipment failure or abnormal operation of the active heave compensation system.

v) When computer based control systems are used for motion compensation systems, they are to comply with the req

ation of the active heave compensation system.

v) When computer based control systems are used for motion compensation systems, they are to comply with the requirements of Section 4-9-3 of the Marine Vessel Rules, as applicable. For the handling of unmanned objects/loads, the motion compensation systems are to comply with the requirements for Category I Systems in accordance with 4-9-3/7.1 TABLE 1. For the handling of manned units (personnel lifting), the motion compensation systems are to comply with the requirements for Category II Systems in accordance with 4-9-3/7.1 TABLE 1.

vi) When active heave compensation systems use synthetic fiber ropes, means are to be provided for monitoring the temperature of the rope. The operating temperature of the rope is to be in accordance with the rope manufacturer's specification. Where there is potential for rope overheating, means are to be provided for cooling the section of rope in-way of the active heave compensation system.

29.7 Testing

Motion compensation systems are to meet the following testing requirements in addition to the requirements of Subsection 20/43:

29.7.1 Manufacturer Testing

Motion compensation systems are to be teste

he following testing requirements in addition to the requirements of Subsection 20/43:

29.7.1 Manufacturer Testing

Motion compensation systems are to be tested to the satisfaction of the Surveyor in accordance with the manufacturer specified factory acceptance testing requirements.

For active heave compensation systems, the testing is to also include simulated testing (such as by using a simulation program to simulate the sensor feedback to the control system) in order to demonstrate the ability of the heave compensation system to maintain the position of the suspended load with reference to a fixed reference frame, under the worst case operating conditions.

29.7.2 On-Board Functional Testing

After installation of the handling system on the vessel, the motion compensation systems are to be functionally tested to the satisfaction of the Surveyor in accordance with the manufacturer's testing requirements. This testing need not be conducted to the rated load of the handling system.

31 Structural Members

Structural members are not to be less than 6.4 mm (0.25 in.) thick and are to be suitably protected from corrosion.

33 Structural Materials

33.1 General

Structural materials

s are not to be less than 6.4 mm (0.25 in.) thick and are to be suitably protected from corrosion.

33 Structural Materials

33.1 General

Structural materials are to be suitable for the intended service conditions. They are to be of good quality, free of injurious defects and are to exhibit satisfactory formability and weldability characteristics. Materials used in the construction of the handling systems are to be certified by the mill and verified by ABS Surveyors. Material is to be clearly identified by the steel manufacturer with the specification, grade and heat number.

33.3 Toughness

For handling systems with design service temperature of -10°C (14°F) and colder, primary structural members such as those listed in 20/35 are to be in conformity with the toughness criteria in 20/37. For systems with design service temperature warmer than -10°C (14°F), primary structural members are to have fracture toughness satisfactory for the intended application as evidenced by previous satisfactory service experience or appropriate toughness tests similar to those in 20/37.

33.5 Additional Requirements

In cases where principal loads from either service or weld residual stresses are imp

e toughness tests similar to those in 20/37.

33.5 Additional Requirements

In cases where principal loads from either service or weld residual stresses are imposed perpendicular to the material thickness, the use of special material with improved through thickness (Z direction) properties is required. Material complying with 2-1-1/17 of the ABS Rules for Materials and Welding (Part 2) is considered as meeting this requirement.

33.7 Steel

Materials, test specimens, and mechanical testing procedures having characteristics differing from those prescribed herein may be approved upon application, due regard being given to established practices in the country in which the material is produced and the purpose for which the material is intended. Wrought iron is not to be used.

33.9 Other Material

Materials other than steel will be specially considered.

33.11 Fasteners (2013)

Fasteners (including bolts, studs and nuts) subjected to tensile loading (other than pre-tensioning), employed in joining of components in the primary load path of handling systems are to be selected to meet strength and corrosion resistance requirements suitable for the intended service and are to be in accorda

ad path of handling systems are to be selected to meet strength and corrosion resistance requirements suitable for the intended service and are to be in accordance with a recognized national or international standard for fasteners. Round bottom or rolled thread profiles are to be used for fasteners in the primary load path of the handling system. Based on the design service temperature, the fracture toughness of these fasteners is to be as per 20/33.3 or 20/37, as applicable.

High-Strength Steel Fasteners: Zinc or Aluminum coated fasteners or Cadmium plated fasteners of tensile strength greater than 150 ksi are not be used in applications exposed to the weather, or where they may be subjected to periodic wetting or heavy condensation. (Under wet conditions, these coatings/platings increase the susceptibility of the fasteners to hydrogen embrittlement).

43.1 Surveyor Attendance

Certification of launch and recovery systems will require attendance of the Surveyor at the plants of the supplier of component parts of the system to ensure proper quality control procedures are in effect. The number and frequency of these visits is to be as the Surveyor may require.

43.3 Static Load Tests (2010)

A static test load of 100% of the vertical design load is to be applied to the structural components of the completed handling system in the presence of the S

A static test load of 100% of the vertical design load is to be applied to the structural components of the completed handling system in the presence of the Surveyor.

43.5 Original Tests on Handling Systems

43.5.1 Loose Gear

43.5.1(a) Tests.

All chains, rings, hooks, links, shackles, swivels, and blocks of the handling system are to be tested in the presence of the Surveyor with a proof load at least equal to the values in 20/55.1 TABLE 2.

43.5.1(b) Examination.

After tests, gear is to be examined with the sheaves and the pins removed for the purpose of determining whether vital parts have been permanently deformed by the test.

43.5.1(c) Certificates.

Articles of loose gear are to have a certificate written by the Surveyor. The certificate is to contain the distinguishing number or mark applied to the article or gear, a description of the particular article or gear, the material specification, date of tests, proof load applied and safe working load. These data are to be attached to the Record of Certification.

43.5.2 Rope Test

Each rope is to have a certificate of test furnished by the manufacturer, supplier, or the Surveyor indicating the load at which a test sample br

5.2 Rope Test

Each rope is to have a certificate of test furnished by the manufacturer, supplier, or the Surveyor indicating the load at which a test sample broke. This certificate is to show size of rope in inches, number of strands, number of wires per strand, quality of wires, and date of test, and is to be attached to the Record of Certification.

45 Functional Test (2015)

Prior to the system being placed in service, the system is to be tested through its full working range with a load equal to 125 percent of the rated load in the presence of the Surveyor. Satisfactory operation of power drives, emergency lifting devices, and brakes is to be demonstrated. After being tested, the system with all its components is to be examined visually for permanent deformation and failure. A copy of the certificate of tests witnessed and issued by the Surveyor is to be attached to the Record of Certification.

47 Operation (2011)

The complete handling system is to operate satisfactorily when handling the rated load at the rated speed.

49 Repairs and Alterations

Alterations, significant repairs and component renewals, are to be carried out under the supervision and to the satisfaction of the Surveyor. Tests and examinations are to be carried out as deemed necessary by the Surveyor. Reports of these tests and examinations are to be placed in the Record of Certification.

51 Running Rope Maintenance Program

51.1 Wire Rope Replacement or Ends Exchange (2021)

The ends of wire rope are to be exchanged every twelve months and the rope is to be maintained as recommended by the manufacturer. As an alternative to end-to-end exchanging of the rope ends, wire ropes are to be replaced every two years. Records of the replacement or exchange are to be maintained as part of the Record of Certification.

51.3 Annual Testing (2021)

Annually, a section is to be removed from the used end of the rope. The length of this section is to be the running distance from the drum through the system and terminating with the end on the deck. A sample from the remaining rope is to be tested to determine its breaking strength. When the breaking strength is less than 5.0 times the design load of the system for wire rope, 7.0 times the desi

be tested to determine its breaking strength. When the breaking strength is less than 5.0 times the design load of the system for wire rope, 7.0 times the design load for fiber or synthetic rope (except nylon), and 9.0 times the design load for nylon rope, the rope is to be renewed.

Following each ends exchange, except the first exchange, the testing as referenced above is to be conducted.

51.5 Lubrication of Wire Rope (2021)

Where applicable, records of lubrication application as per the manufacturer's recommendations are to be maintained as part of the Record of Certification.

Certificate for Handling Systems (see 1A-7-2/5 of the ABS Rules for Conditions of Classification (Part 1A)).

Certificates for items of loose gear (see 20/43.5.1).

Certificates for rope components (see 20/43.5.2).

Report of

r Conditions of Classification (Part 1A)).

Certificates for rope components (see 20/43.5.2).

Report of tests and surveys during construction (see 20/43.1 and 20/43.3).

Report of initial tests and examinations to system as a unit (see 20/45).

Report of tests and examinations following repairs or design modifications (see 20/49).

Rope maintenance program (see 20/51).

material and dimensions, valves and overpressure protective devices, pressure reducing valves for all control piping systems
