1.7 Basis of Review

The basis of the review by ABS will be the Sections of these Rules and the Marine Vessel Rules as applicable (or other recognized standards provided they are not less effective) and the following requirements.

3 Location of Dive Control Station

A dive control station may be located on the shore or on an offshore platform close to and in sight of the diving location.

The position of the dive control station is to allow the operations control personnel an overview of all systems and activities associated with the operations of the underwater vehicle and the dive. It is not to be located in hazardous areas.

When selecting the location of the dive control station, ship's motion or support structure vibrations are to be considered.

The dive control station is to be provided with air conditioning for control consoles when required by the operational characteristics of electronic components within the consoles.

The leading of pipes in the vicinity of control consoles is to be avoided as far as possible.

When such leads are necessary, care is to be taken in order t

s.

The leading of pipes in the vicinity of control consoles is to be avoided as far as possible.

When such leads are necessary, care is to be taken in order to fit no flange or joints over or near the consoles, or stands, unless provision is made to prevent any leakage from injuring equipment.

The dive control station is to be provided with effective fire protection on all delimiting walls, bulkheads and decks.

5 Construction and Mechanical Protection

All enclosures in the dive control station are to be drip-proof and corrosion resistant when completed and are to be made of one or a combination of the following materials:

Cast metal, except die-cast metal, at least 3 mm (1 / 8 in.) thick at every location.

Non-metallic materials that have acceptable strength, and are non-combustible and non-absorptive (e.g., laminated phenolic material).

Sheet metal of adequate strength. The supporting framework for all panels is to be of rigid construction. No wood is to be used, except for hardwood for non-conducting handrails.

The dive control station is to be located in a dry place. Clear working space is to be provided around panels, consoles and stands to enable doors to be fully ope

e dive control station is to be located in a dry place. Clear working space is to be provided around panels, consoles and stands to enable doors to be fully opened and equipment removed for maintenance and replacement. Consoles, panels and stands are to be firmly secured to a solid foundation, be selfsupported or be braced to the bulkheads.

7 Enclosed Dive Control Stations

Enclosed dive control stations are to have two means of access located as remote from each other as practicable.

Glass windows in the control station are to be of shatter-resistant type.

Section 21 Dive Control Stations 21

Sufficient light fixtures are to be installed to provide 540 lumens/m2 (50 foot-candles) over all control stands, consoles and panels.

9 Controls, Displays and Alarms

9.1 General

Controls, displays and alarms are to provide for safe and reliable performance of all the required functions carried out from the dive control station.

Fire detection and fire fighting systems are to be provided for the protection of the station and are to be operable from outside the protected spaces.

Controls for fire fighting systems intended for the protection of diving facilities (e.g., deck decompressio

to be operable from outside the protected spaces.

Controls for fire fighting systems intended for the protection of diving facilities (e.g., deck decompression chambers, handling systems) are to be located in or as close as possible to the control station.

9.3 Control Consoles

All controls, displays and alarms are to be located and arranged in centralized positions and constructed in accordance with practices suitable for the service.

A separate control console is to be provided for each independently operated deck decompression chamber and underwater unit, and its handling system.

9.5 Displays and Alarms

The following operating parameters are to be monitored at the dive control station for each manned chamber and underwater unit:

Pressure or depth

Temperature

Humidity

Partial oxygen pressure

Partial CO2 pressure

Pressure of connected breathing gas bottles

Pressure at pressure reducing outlets

Oxygen content in supply lines to chamber and compartment and to breathing masks

Battery charge and discharge, voltmeter, ammeter and a capacity indicator

Power supply distributions, voltmeter, ammeter and frequency meter if alternating current is used

Electric leakage indic

ter, ammeter and a capacity indicator

Power supply distributions, voltmeter, ammeter and frequency meter if alternating current is used

Electric leakage indicator for all chambers and compartments

Fire alarm display panels

Safety and signaling system monitors

Display and control for breathing mixtures

Environmental systems controls including heating and cooling system controls

11 Communications

Direct communication is to be provided among the following positions:

Dive control station

Section 21 Dive Control Stations 21

Dive control console on the support vessel

Winch and crane local operation stand

All compartments associated with saturation diving

Master of the diving support vessel

Underwater vehicle

Diver in the water

Automatic recording of communication between the submersible and the control station is to be possible.

IMO – Diving Bell Emergency Locating Device

(2009) Code of Safety for Diving Systems, Para. 2.12.5 of IMO Res. A.831(19).

A diving bell should have as emergency locating device with a frequency of 37.5 kHz designed to assist personnel on the surface in establishing and maintaining contact with the submerged diving bell if the umbilical to the surface is severed. The device should include the following components:

.1 Transponder

.1.1

The transponder should be provided with a pressure housing capable of operating to a depth of at least 2

nclude the following components:

.1 Transponder

.1.1

The transponder should be provided with a pressure housing capable of operating to a depth of at least 200 m containing batteries and equipped with salt water activation contacts. The batteries should be of the readily available "alkaline" type and, if possible, be interchangeable with those of the diver and surface interrogator receiver.

.1.2

The transponder should be designed to operate with the following characteristics:

Common emergency reply frequency 37.5 kHz

Individual interrogation frequencies:

Channel A channel B 38.5 ±0.05 kHz 39.5 ±0.05 kHz

Receiver sensitivity +15 db referred to 1 μbar

Minimum interrogation pulse width 4 ms

Turnaround delay 125.7 ±0.2 ms Reply frequency 37.5 ±0.05 kHz

Maximum interrogation rates:

more than 20 percent of battery life remaining Once per second less than 20 percent of battery life remaining Once per 2 seconds

Minimum transponder output power 85 db referred to 1 μbar at 1 m

Minimum transducer polar diagram -6 db at ±135° solid angle, centered on the transponder vertical axis and

transmitting towards the surface

Minimum listening life in water 10 weeks Minimum battery life

135° solid angle, centered on the transponder vertical axis and

transmitting towards the surface

Minimum listening life in water 10 weeks Minimum battery life replying at 85 db 5 days

.2 Diver-held Interrogator/Receiver

.2.1

The interrogator/receiver should be provided with a pressure housing capable of operating to a depth of at least 200 m with pistol grip and compass. The front end should contain the directional hydrophone array and the rear end the 3-digit LED display readout calibrated in meters. Controls should be provided for "on/off receiver gain" and "channel selection". The battery pack should be of the readily available "alkaline" type and, if possible, be interchangeable with that of the interrogator and transponder.

.2.2 (2009)

The interrogator/receiver should be designed to operate with the following characteristics:

Common emergency reply frequency 37.5 kHz

Individual interrogation frequencies:

channel A channel B 38.5 kHz 39.5 kHz

Minimum transmitter output power 85 db referred to 1 μbar at 1 m

Transmit pulse 4 ms Directivity ±15°

Capability to zero range on transponder

Maximum detectable range more than 500 m

ansmit pulse 4 ms Directivity ±15°

2.12.6

In addition to the communication systems referred to above, a standard bell emergency communication tapping code should be adopted as given below for use between persons in the bell and rescue divers. A copy of this code should be displayed inside and outside the bell and also in the dive control room.

Bell Emergency Communication Tapping Code

DNV Rules for Classification of DP Systems

Development, Present Focus Areas and Future Directions

Author: Aleks Karlsen, DNV Venue: MTS DP 2013 Luncheon Presentation, Houston Date: October 2013 Document Type: Conference Presentation (21 slides)

Agenda

Background

DNV Rule Development Process

DNV Rules and

ABS Guide for Dynamic Positioning Systems

Publisher: American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) Edition: February 2024 Document Type: Classification Guide Applicability: New constructions — vessels, offshore installations and facilities

Overview

The ABS Guide for Dynamic Positioning Systems defines classification requirements for the design, installation, and testing of DP systems. It replaces DP requirements previously contained in the ABS Rules for Building and Classing Marine Vessels (Marine Vessel Rules).

Key Features of the Guide

Enhanced System (EHS) notations — recognizes design features beyond standard DPS-series notations; provides flexibility for owners and operators

Station Keeping Performance (SKP) notations — recognizes DP capability and encourages robust system design

Increased technical detail — supports less experienced users

Vessel type specifics — reflects diverse industry applications

Testing requirements — aligned with current industry practice

Alignment with IMO Guidelines

IMO MSC.1/Circ.1580 (2017) — applicable to vessels constructed on or after 9 June 2017

IMO MSC/Circ.645 (1994) — continues to apply to vessels constructed between 1 July 1994 and 9 June 20

applicable to vessels constructed on or after 9 June 2017

IMO MSC/Circ.645 (1994) — continues to apply to vessels constructed between 1 July 1994 and 9 June 2017

Classification Notations

Standard DPS Notations

Notation Description DPS-0 Basic DP system; no redundancy required DPS-1 Single failure may result in loss of position; limited redundancy DPS-2 No single failure causes loss of position; redundant systems required DPS-3 No single failure including fire or flood in any one compartment causes loss of position

Enhanced System (EHS) Notations

Additional voluntary notations for DPS-2 and DPS-3 vessels:

Notation Focus Area EHS-E Enhanced Electrical System EHS-P Enhanced Power and Thruster System EHS-C Enhanced Control System EHS-F Enhanced Fire and Flood Protection

Station Keeping Performance (SKP) Notations

SKP notations recognize a vessel's documented DP station-keeping capability under defined environmental conditions.

DNV GL DP Capability Standard ST-0111

Challenges and Updates One Year from Publishing

Authors: Luca Pivano, Aleks Karlsen (DNV GL AS) Conference: MTS Dynamic Positioning Conference, Houston Date: October 10–11, 2017 Session: Performance

Abstract

In July 2016, DNV GL published a new standard for the assessment of vessel DP capability — DNVGL-ST-0111. The standard defines three levels of DP Capability assessment (Level 1, 2, and 3) plus site-specific sub-levels. A free web-app supporting Level 1 calculations was released simultaneously.

This paper reports on: - Industry feedback and challenges encountered in the first year of use - Updates to the standard (planned for Q4 2017) - Updates to the web-app - Guidance on selecting the appropriate assessment level

Introduction and History

Background

Reliable documentation of a vessel's station-keeping capability is vital for planning and executing safe DP operations. A vessel's station-keeping capability is a key DP performance indicator — for both intact condition and post-worst-case-failure condition for redundant DP vessels.

Predecessor: DNV GL ERN Concept

ST-0111 is the successor to the environmental regularity number (ern) co

orst-case-failure condition for redundant DP vessels.

Predecessor: DNV GL ERN Concept

ST-0111 is the successor to the environmental regularity number (ern) concept, established in 1977. While the ern concept served the industry well for decades, the general development of DP technology and industry practice made it clear that a new, more rigorous standard was needed.

Publication

DNVGL-ST-0111 was published in July 2016. Primary objective: set clear and consistent requirements for DP station-keeping capability assessment.

Standard Structure — Three Assessment Levels

DP Capability Level 1

Method: Prescriptive static method

Environmental basis: Beaufort weather scale

Applicability: Ship-shaped mono-hulls only

Key property: Results are fully consistent and comparable across different vessels

Tool: Free web-app available at My DNV GL

DP Capability Level 2

Method: Quasi-static method — more comprehensive than Level 1

Environmental basis: Beaufort scale (Level 2) or site-specific (Level 2-Site)

Applicability: All hull forms (mono-hulls, semi-submersibles, catamarans, etc.)

Flexibility: Allows vessel/project-specific wind, current, wave drift coefficients and actuator chara

l forms (mono-hulls, semi-submersibles, catamarans, etc.)

Flexibility: Allows vessel/project-specific wind, current, wave drift coefficients and actuator characteristics

DP Capability Level 3

Method: Time-domain simulations

Environmental basis: Beaufort scale (Level 3) or site-specific (Level 3-Site)

Provides: Full dynamic information — vessel motions, thruster dynamics, power dynamics, etc.

Site-Specific Sub-Levels

Level 2-Site and Level 3-Site use the same methods as Level 2/3, but additionally allow: - Site-specific environmental data (replacing the Beaufort scale) - Inclusion of external forces (risers, cables, pipes)

Guidance on Assessment Level Selection

Level Typical Use Value Level 1 High-level DP capability assessment (chartering, early design phase); vessel benchmarking; oil company tender requirements Prescriptive method → consistent and comparable results; free web-app; easy to deliver class-required numbers Level 2 When vessel-specific parameters are important; hull forms other than mono-hulls Takes advantage of vessel/equipment-specific performance Level 2-Site Site-specific environmental conditions (shallow water, high current); external forces (risers, cabl
entities:
Assessment Level Selection

Level Typical Use Value Level

nodes.content:
antage of vessel/equipment-specific performance Level 2-Site Site-specific environmental conditions (shallow water, high current); external forces (risers, cables) Site-specific weather + external forces; preliminary operational planning Level 3 / Level 3-Site More detailed vessel performance information; vessel and equipment dynamics important; critical operations planning Designers: right-size vessels, realistic fuel consumption, battery sizing; Operators: improved planning and risk control; Owners: proof of suitability for specific operations

Feedback from Industry — First Year Experience

Experiences from Practical Use

Users: Ship designers, vessel owners, consultants, oil companies, equipment vendors, research institutions, universities.

Overall feedback: Very positive. However, several improvement areas were identified.

Standard — Key Feedback

Skeg thrust forbidden sectors: The original standard created a thrust forbidden zone where thrusters could not produce net force near a skeg. For vessels where one bow tunnel thruster and one main azimuth remain operational after worst-case failure, the skeg prohibition often prevented the main azimuth from pushing toward the skeg

l thruster and one main azimuth remain operational after worst-case failure, the skeg prohibition often prevented the main azimuth from pushing toward the skeg — resulting in zero capability for some headings (D = 0 in the DP Capability string).

Resolution in new version: Thruster-skeg interaction is now modelled as a thrust loss factor, consistent with industrial DP control system practice and providing more realistic results.

Other feedback: - Unclear definitions and wording in several sections - Most suggestions are incorporated in the new release

Web-App — Key Feedback

Many users requested Level 2 and Level 2-Site in the web-app (only Level 1 was initially available)

Request for vessel comparison feature (up to 4 vessels simultaneously)

Request for smaller heading resolution (down to 2 degrees, for Norwegian Continental Shelf shuttle tanker requirements)

Request for improved inline help and user manual

Comments on Application of the Standard

Worst Case Single Failure (WCSF) definition: Defining and agreeing on the WCSF — especially for complex or flexible systems with many redundancy groups — has been challenging. The new standard places greater emphasis on proper WCSF

he WCSF — especially for complex or flexible systems with many redundancy groups — has been challenging. The new standard places greater emphasis on proper WCSF analysis.

Specification challenges: - Some operators initially required both the new ST-0111 and the old ern concept in parallel (to gain experience with the new standard results) - Some specifications used Level 1 (Beaufort-based) for site-specific North Sea conditions — an incorrect application - Industry needs time to adapt to new standards; guidance on specification writing is important

Conservatism compared to older methods: Some existing vessels receive relatively lower capability numbers under ST-0111 compared to older methodologies. This reflects the removal of flexibility that allowed inconsistent results under older standards. The new standard provides better benchmarking, even if slightly more conservative.

Key conclusion: Users of the results must evaluate the assessment relative to the actual DP capability needed for their operations — not simply select the vessel with the highest capability numbers or largest limiting wind speed.

Classification applicability: DNV GL does not require DP Capability assessmen

ct the vessel with the highest capability numbers or largest limiting wind speed.

Classification applicability: DNV GL does not require DP Capability assessment for existing vessels as part of classification. ST-0111 is required only for new buildings classed under DNV GL per the January 2017 edition of RU-SHIPS and onwards.

Updates in the New Revision of the Standard

Changes Proposed for Q4 2017 Release

DP Capability Numbers format — corrected to use the smallest DP Capability per heading sector (previous version erroneously used the largest)

Verification requirements — improved to include a check on both actuator forces and environmental forces in the analysis report

Water plane area coefficient — values outside the valid range of the Level 1 method are now capped at the maximum/minimum allowed value

Propeller diameter for contra-rotating pod actuators — defined as the diameter of the largest propeller

Efficiency of Cycloidal actuators — efficiency factor increased from 850 to 900 (based on additional documentation review)

Propeller submergence definition — rewritten for clarity; a formula is now provided

Thrust losses due to thruster-skeg interaction — major update; th

review)

Propeller submergence definition — rewritten for clarity; a formula is now provided

Thrust losses due to thruster-skeg interaction — major update; thruster-skeg interaction now produces a thrust loss factor rather than a forbidden sector

Average wind speed for site analyses — 1-minute average wind speed recommended for Level 2-Site and Level 3-Site analyses, consistent with industry practice

Thrust ventilation loss formulation — confirmed applicable with JONSWAP wave spectrum and directional spreading 2–8 (per DNV-RP-C205, section 3.5.8.4)

Updates in the New Release of the Web-App

Improved guidance and user error handling — more inline help, input field validation (range checks, dropdown menus)

Smaller heading resolution — configurable down to 2-degree resolution (supports NCS shuttle tanker requirements)

Vessel comparison — compare up to 4 vessels simultaneously (same run type required for comparison)

Level 2 and Level 2-Site implemented — users can now input:

Vessel-specific wind, current, and wave drift coefficients

Vessel-specific actuator characteristics

Site-specific environmental conditions and wave spectra (JONSWAP and Pierson-Moskowitz)

Standard updat

coefficients

Vessel-specific actuator characteristics

Site-specific environmental conditions and wave spectra (JONSWAP and Pierson-Moskowitz)

Standard updates incorporated — web-app reflects all changes in the new standard revision

Bug fixes — including a fix preventing vessel import when the exported file contained configuration errors

DP Capability Result Format

Level 1 Result String Example

Results are expressed as a string of Beaufort numbers representing the minimum limiting wind speed in four heading sectors (typically every 10 degrees):

DP Capability L1 (10, 8, 7, 5)

Displayed as a polar wind envelope plot

Each number = limiting Beaufort wind speed for that heading sector

The smallest value per sector is used (corrected in new version)

Conclusion

DNVGL-ST-0111 has been well received by the industry. One year of feedback has identified several improvement areas, primarily:

Skeg-thruster interaction modelling

Web-app usability and Level 2/2-Site availability

WCSF definition complexity

Specification guidance

The updated standard (Q4 2017) and updated web-app address these issues. The Level 2/Level 2-Site implementation in the web-app significantly extends the

dance

The updated standard (Q4 2017) and updated web-app address these issues. The Level 2/Level 2-Site implementation in the web-app significantly extends the tool's applicability to non-mono-hull vessels and site-specific operations.

Acknowledgements

Eivind Ruth (DNV GL) — hydrodynamic calculations for thruster-skeg interaction and propeller ventilation thrust loss updates.

References

A. Karlsen, L. Pivano — DP Capability ST-0111 Webinar, March 2017.

ssessment of the Station-Keeping Capability of DP Vessels," MTS DP Conference, October 2016

DNV Rules for Classification of DP Systems

Development, Present Focus Areas and Future Directions

Author: Aleks Karlsen, DNV Venue: MTS DP 2013 Luncheon Presentation, Houston Date: October 2013 Document Type: Conference Presentation (21 slides)

Agenda

Background

DNV Rule Development Process

DNV Rules and Standards Related to DP

Development Within DP Rules

Present Challenges Influencing DP Rule Development

Possible Future Rule Development

Present Focus Areas (including power systems with closed bus-ties)

Summary

Rule Development Philosophy

DNV rule development is driven by the objective of safeguarding life, property and the environment (consistent with MTS's goal of incident-free operations). Key principles:

Industry support — technically supporting safe operations

Cooperation — with industry, authorities, universities and research institutions

Manageable pace — neither too fast nor too slow

Key drivers: incidents and accidents, politics, IMO and other regulators

Consistency and transparency are paramount

DNV Rule Development Process

The standard process takes 15 to 24 months from initiation to entry into force:

Need for rule change identified

Get funding

Wri

DNV Rule Development Process

The standard process takes 15 to 24 months from initiation to entry into force:

Need for rule change identified

Get funding

Write proposal

Internal hearing

Internal comments concluded → Update proposal

External hearing

External comments concluded → Update proposal

Approval of rule proposal

Rules published

Rules in force (0.5 year after publication)

Historical Perspective: DP Class 2 System Characteristics

Early Days (circa 1980s–1990s)

(Based on Chris Jenman, Global Maritime, "DP Past, Present and Future", MTS DP Conference 2011)

No single fault shall cause a catastrophic failure

Never exceed vessel's capabilities

Capable personnel

FMEA

Withstand the loss of one thruster

Withstand the loss of one diesel generator

Withstand the loss of one switchboard

No failure to full thrust for any propeller (mostly CPP vessels)

Three position references: 2 on-line + 1 standby

Redundant DP control system, or non-redundant + Joystick

Today's High-End Class 2 and Class 3 Standards

Evolved requirements compared to early days:

No single fault shall cause loss of position or heading (to avoid catastrophic events)

Never exceed vessel's sub-failur

d requirements compared to early days:

No single fault shall cause loss of position or heading (to avoid catastrophic events)

Never exceed vessel's sub-failure capabilities, continuously monitored by DP Consequence Analysis

Capable certified personnel with higher focus on operational aspects

Designated workstations for DP operations

FMEA to a higher standard, based on multiple technical system operational modes

Withstand the worst case relevant failure mode (not just a specific component)

Systems split into more than two redundancy groups

More complex power systems; desire to operate with closed bus-ties

No failure to full thrust for any propeller (RPM control is state of the art; easier to mitigate than CPP)

At least three position references: all on-line

Redundant DP control system + Joystick + Manual levers

Back-up non-redundant DP control system for DP class 3

DNV DP-Related Rules and Standards Portfolio (as of 2013)

Document Description DYNPOS DNV main DP class notations (DYNPOS-1, -2, -3, AUTR, AUTRO) DPS IMO minimum requirements (DPS-1, DPS-2, DPS-3) ER (Enhanced Reliability) DNV new DP principles for enhanced redundancy design ISDS Integrated Software Dependen

IMO minimum requirements (DPS-1, DPS-2, DPS-3) ER (Enhanced Reliability) DNV new DP principles for enhanced redundancy design ISDS Integrated Software Dependent Systems HIL-IS / HIL-DS Enhanced System Verification via Hardware-In-the-Loop simulation RP for DP Design Recommended Practice for DP system design RP for DP Operations Recommended Practice for DP operations (DNV-RP-E307) RP for FMEA Recommended Practice for FMEA of redundant systems (DNV-RP-D102)

Present Challenges Influencing DP Rule Development

Constant Development at High Pace

More suppliers of DP equipment and DP vessels

New operations in new and challenging environments (e.g., Arctic)

New designs and technologies (e.g., environmental friendliness)

New ways to apply existing technology

More integration and automation

Closed bus-ties

New verification tools and methods

Complex and flexible systems — verification and testability is a challenge

Tension between safety and environmental objectives

Continuously evolving rules and standards

Market Growth

Growth in both high-end and low-end DP vessel segments

High-end operators set very strong requirements on class

Other parts of the market are primarily focuse

Growth in both high-end and low-end DP vessel segments

High-end operators set very strong requirements on class

Other parts of the market are primarily focused on obtaining the certificate

Overarching Need

Clear, up-to-date rules applied consistently across the industry.

Possible Future Rule Development Focus Areas

Introduction of new verification tools and methods

Continued focus on closed bus-tie systems — generators from different redundancy groups running in parallel

New standards for DP Capacity verification (including under WCF conditions)

Higher focus on FMEA verification and testing

Higher focus on Online Consequence Analysis, reflecting operational flexibility and limitations

More vessel/vessel-type specific requirements

Rules for new technologies (e.g., batteries)

Higher focus on integration of control systems and software-dependent systems

Better alignment of class annual trial scope with industry/IMO guidelines

New initiatives are typically introduced first as Recommended Practices or guideline documents, then escalated to: - Voluntary additional class notations - Main class requirements

New Verification Tools and Methods (ESV)

DNV identifies the need f

uments, then escalated to: - Voluntary additional class notations - Main class requirements

New Verification Tools and Methods (ESV)

DNV identifies the need for basic research and further development on verification methodologies and services. The rationale is twofold:

Different conditions: Modern DP vessels operate in environments far more demanding than vessels of the past

Different toolbox: Digital tools (HIL simulators, model-based engineering, etc.) have replaced traditional physical testing methods in many domains

Verification Framework — "Test Smarter"

Verification methods span three dimensions — Process, Product, and Performance — applied across the full system lifecycle:

Lifecycle Phase Key Verification Activities Concept ISDS, MBE, SQALE Design CMMI-RP, ISO 17894, IEC 61508 Test & Commissioning HIL, FMEA, Main Class requirements Sea Trials FSA-Risk, Risk-based verification Operation Safety Case, SCM, Model Me

DP Power System Trends — Closed Bus Operations

Traditional Split-Bus Architecture

Generators and thrusters divided into two or more independent redundancy groups

Bus-ties normally open — each group operates independently

Loss of one bus does not affect th
entities: Lifecycle Phase Key Verification Activities Concept

nodes.content:
thrusters divided into two or more independent redundancy groups

Bus-ties normally open — each group operates independently

Loss of one bus does not affect the other

Standard for DYNPOS-2 and DYNPOS-3

Modern Trend: Closed Bus Operation

All generators connected to a single common bus

Benefits: improved fuel efficiency, reduced emissions, lower mechanical wear

Challenge: a fault on the bus (e.g., short circuit) can affect all generators simultaneously — threatening the fundamental redundancy principle

Requires sophisticated protection systems, discrimination logic, and fault ride-through capability

DP-3 Closed Bus Requirements (2013 Status)

To achieve equivalent integrity to DP-3 open bus-tie systems, closed bus DP-3 requires:

Generator Protection (GP) systems — detecting failures not addressed by traditional generator and switchboard protection, preventing blackouts

Live short circuit testing at the main switchboard level:

3-phase short circuit

1-phase short circuit

Earth faults

Comprehensive failure mode analysis and FMEA testing

Power System Integrity Spectrum (2013)

100% ← DP-3 open bus-ties
       ← DP-3 closed bus (2013) [gap must be minimized per IMO 645 equi

ysis and FMEA testing

Power System Integrity Spectrum (2013)

100% ← DP-3 open bus-ties
       ← DP-3 closed bus (2013) [gap must be minimized per IMO 645 equivalence]
       ← DP-2 closed bus specification (2013)
       ← DP-2 variable closed bus integrity
1A1   ← Basic class (minimal DP)
0%

Key FMEA Failure Modes for Closed Bus DP Systems

DP-2 Minimum Analysis Requirements

Failure Mode Required Analysis / Testing Short circuits, earth failures Discrimination analysis between generator breakers and bus-ties Overload Handled by PMS/DP/switchboard logic; FMEA + functional testing Governor failures Analyse and test: power failure, speed pick-up failure, overspeed, isochronous load sharing Overvoltage / AVR failures FMEA analysis; test: loss of excitation, over-excitation PMS and active load sharing Analysis + functional testing; FMEA test of I/O failures and load sharing line failures Transient undervoltage (short circuit ride-through) Analyse effect of transient voltage dips; verify ride-through without operator intervention

DP-3 Additional Requirements

Generator Protection (GP) systems mandatory

Live short circuit tests at main switchboard level

Short circuit tests may exte
entities:
Minimum Analysis Requirements

Failure Mode Required Analysis

nodes.content:
ion

DP-3 Additional Requirements

Generator Protection (GP) systems mandatory

Live short circuit tests at main switchboard level

Short circuit tests may extend to all power systems (AC, DC, UPS) crossing redundancy group boundaries

Transient Undervoltage — Key Concerns

The FMEA must evaluate whether transient voltage dips can cause: - Unintended tripping of frequency converters - Unintended deselection of thrusters from DP control - Unintended tripping of motors (including auxiliary pumps) - Unintended activation of undervoltage protection - Interruption to DC control power systems

Requirement: Equipment not in the affected redundancy group must ride through the transient and be immediately available when voltage is re-established, without operator intervention.

FMEA Reference: DNV-RP-D102

Detailed failure mode lists for DP systems operating with closed bus-ties are provided in DNV Recommended Practice DNV-RP-D102 (January 2012, Appendix D).

Additional failure modes to analyse include: 1. Active and reactive load sharing failures 2. Consequences of voltage transients 3. Risk of thruster load reduction during transients 4. Hidden failures preventing bus-tie opening 5. Fault

aring failures 2. Consequences of voltage transients 3. Risk of thruster load reduction during transients 4. Hidden failures preventing bus-tie opening 5. Fault tolerance in the PMS system 6. Short circuit selectivity between bus-ties and generator breakers 7. Mode monitoring in PMS/IAS systems 8. Loop monitoring (e.g., similar to PMS) 9. Bus-tie breaker shunt-trip capability

Summary

DNV's approach to DP rule development is characterized by:

Keeping pace with industry development — technically, operationally, and politically

Pushing and pulling toward improved minimum requirements

Active cooperation with industry in setting new standards

Consistency and transparency in rule application

The central near-term focus areas are closed bus-tie power systems and new verification methodologies, reflecting the industry's move toward more integrated, fuel-efficient, and complex DP system architectures.
